records.content:
A Computational Study of Belief, Surprise, and Structural Inference Under Uncertainty

This documentation accompanies the TAMBI research project, which investigates how humans infer latent environmental structure under different levels of task ambiguity, and how evolving beliefs guide spatio-temporal action under uncertainty. The study integrates a custom behavioral task, multi-modal physiological recording and multiple questionnaires covering mental health, focusing anxiety. A family of 15 particle-filter based agent models provide a tangible representation of how differences in prior knowledge, cognitive capabilities and decision-making preferences shape behavior in our task. Building up on that, we use armortized bayesian inference to extract four individual parameters that reflect the underlying trait-level differences in behavioral patterns. By correlating(?) these parameters with the questionnaire scores, individual task performace differences and related affective reactions, we build a unified framework for the investigation of how mental health profiles are linked to learning and inference in uncertain dynamic environments.

records.content:
This book is organized into five major parts:

Theoretical Background
A concise review of the conceptual foundations motivating the project: the decomposition of uncertainty into epistemic and aleatoric components, the active inference framework, and the computational mechanisms by which trait anxiety is hypothesized to distort inference and learning.

The Experiment
A full description of the experimental design, the Mosquito Defense task, the trial schedule generation procedure, and the PsychoPy implementation. This section covers the three instruction conditions, the gamification layer, and the multi-modal data streams collected during testing.

The Simulation Models
Detailed specifications for all 15 computational models, organized into four conceptual groups: baseline models, core Bayesian inference models, temporal commitment models, and rule-knowledge models. This section also covers the shared model architecture (particle filter, action selection, cost function), implementation details, and simulation results.

Participant Data Analysis
Preprocessing and analysis pipelines for behavioral, eye-tracking, pupil, mouse-tracking, and heart rate data. Includes the post-experiment questionnaire analysis.

Computational Model Fitting
The Amortized Bayesian Inference procedure for participant-level parameter inference, including parameter recovery, and identifiability analyses.

records.content:
The core experimental paradigm - the Mosquito Defense Task - requires participants to allocate a fixed budget of seven nets across 32 angular slots surrounding a central target, in order to intercept probabilistic mosquito attacks. The direction of each attack is governed by latent contextual states and color cues, according to rules that participants may or may not be explicitly taught (depending on instruction condition). Episodes of approximately 20 trials (16-24) each are separated by unannounced rotations of the entire cue-outcome mapping.

The task is designed to place varying demands on:

Cue-contingency learning - learning which color predicts which direction

Latent state inference - tracking which of two rotated contexts is currently active

Uncertainty decomposition - distinguishing irreducible outcome noise from genuine structural change

Fifteen particle-filter-based computational models varying systematically in representational capacity, inferential mechanisms, and prior knowledge are simulated on the task to identify different related behavioral patterns. The models span the range from a memoryless Markov baseline to an oracle agent with complete structural knowledge, with the primary scientific interest lying in the intermediate inference models and how structural priors impact their behavior.

Using Amortized Bayesian Inference we estimate four participant-level parameters that reflect
By mapping the individual parameter values on questionnaire scores and physiological measures, this approach allows to investigate how individual differences in model parameters relate to trait anxiety and other individual difference variables.

Documentation version: April 2026

Formally, we seek the posterior:p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto p(\mathbf{x} \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}) \cdot p(\boldsymbol{\theta})

where \boldsymbol{\theta} = (\text{pw}, \text{nr}, \text{bm}, h) \in \mathbb{R}^4 are the cognitive parameters and \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{40} is the feature vector. Rather than computing this posterior analytically or via sampling, we train a conditional density estimator q_\phi(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid \mathbf{x}) parameterized by neural network weights \phi to approximate p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid \mathbf{x}) across the full joint space (\boldsymbol{\theta}, \mathbf{x}).

We approximated the posterior using neural posterior estimation (NPE) with learned summary statistics (

Radev et al., 2020), as implemented in the BayesFlow 2.0 framework for amortized Bayesian inference (

Radev et al., 2023a).

Specificially, the implemented inference pipeline uses the following architecture:



Figure 1:The Amortized Bayesian Inference Workflow.Agent Simulation → Feature Extraction → DeepSet(64) → FlowMatching → Posterior
     (CPU)            (deterministic)     (summary)    (inference)   (4 params)

Summary Network: A DeepSet (

Zaheer et al., 2017) maps the 40-dimensional feature vector (reshaped to a 1-element set) to a 64-dimensional summary representation. The DeepSet architecture is used (instead of an MLP) to comply with BayesFlow’s internal architecture requirements for condition passing.\mathbf{h} = \text{DeepSet}(\mathbf{x}) \in \mathbb{R}^{64}

Inference Network: FlowMatching (

Lipman et al., 2022; 

2024) with optimal transport and an MLP subnet (4 layers × 256 units, 5% dropout) learns a conditional normalizing flow from the 64-dimensional summary to the 4-dimensional posterior over parameters. FlowMatching was chosen over coupling flows for its faster convergence and better handling of multimodal posteriors.

How this works: the 64-dimensional summary \mathbf{h} conditions a Flow Matching model that learns a continuous normalizing flow from a standard normal base distribution to the conditional posterior:\boldsymbol{\theta} = F_\phi(\mathbf{z}; \mathbf{h}), \quad \mathbf{z} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I}_4)

The flow F_\phi is parameterized by an MLP with 4 hidden layers of 256 units each, 5% dropout, and mish activation. We use optimal transport coupling (

Tong et al., 2023) to improve training efficiency.

The total number of trainable parameters is approximately 850,000.

The network is trained on 100,000 simulated sessions:

Data generation. N_{\text{train}} = 100{,}000 simulations are generated in parallel. For each simulation:

Parameters \boldsymbol{\theta} are drawn from the uniform priors

A trial schedule is randomly selected from the 20 available schedules

The agent runs the full session, producing allocations \{\mathbf{g}_t\}

The 40-dimensional feature vector \mathbf{x} is extracted

The pre-generated feature vectors are then used to train the DeepSet + FlowMatching network:

Training. The network is trained offline (i.e., on the pre-generated dataset, not with on-the-fly simulation) for up to 300 epochs with:

Batch size: 256

Initial learning rate: 5 \times 10^{-4}

Optimizer: Adam (this is the default Keras settings)

Early stopping: patience 50, restoring best weights (monitored on validation loss)

Validation split: 10% (10,000 held-out simulations)

Loss function. BayesFlow’s default training objective for Flow Matching is the conditional flow matching loss (

Lipman et al., 2022; 

2024), which minimizes the expected squared error between the learned and target velocity fields:\mathcal{L}(\phi) = \mathbb{E}_{t, \boldsymbol{\theta}, \mathbf{z}, \mathbf{x}} \left[ \| v_\phi(t, \boldsymbol{\theta}_t; \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x})) - u_t(\boldsymbol{\theta}_t \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}) \|^2 \right]

where t \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1), \boldsymbol{\theta}_t = (1-t)\mathbf{z} + t\boldsymbol{\theta} (with optimal transport pairing), and u_t is the target velocity field.

How our loss curve looks like:



Figure 2:Training Loss and Validation Loss
To ensure both stability and validity of the inference pipeline, we run Simulation-Based Calibration (SBC) and Parameter Recoverability as complementary validation procedures.

Following 

Talts et al. (2018), we verify posterior calibration via SBC. For M = 500 simulations with known parameters the posterior is sampled and the rank of the true parameter within the posterior is computed. Specificially, this is how the procedure works:

Draw \boldsymbol{\theta}^* \sim p(\boldsymbol{\theta}) from the prior

Simulate data \mathbf{x}^* from the agent with \boldsymbol{\theta}^*

Draw K = 1000 posterior samples \{\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(k)}\}_{k=1}^K \sim q_\phi(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid \mathbf{x}^*)

Compute the rank statistic: r_i = \sum_{k=1}^K \mathbb{I}[\theta_i^{(k)} < \theta_i^*] for each parameter i

Under correct posterior calibration, r_i \sim \text{Discrete Uniform}(0, K). Deviations from uniformity indicate systematic bias (mean \neq K/2) or miscalibration (variance \neq K^2/12).

Our SBC results:



Figure 3:The SBC results

We run 300 simulations with known parameters. The posterior mean is compared to the true value. High correlation (r > 0.9) and low RMSE indicate reliable estimation.

Our parameter recovery results:

For real participants, the pipeline:

Parses net allocations from the all_slot_barriers column of the behavioral results CSV

Extracts the trial schedule from the same file (episode, cue_identity, actual_vector_angle, outcome_occurred, is_oddball)

Computes the identical 40 features used during training

Passes the feature vector through the trained network

Returns 4,000 posterior samples per parameter, from which means, standard deviations, and credible intervals are computed*

Clips samples to prior bounds to prevent out-of-support estimates

hierarchy.lvl2: Why we decided to take a feature-based approach
Why we decided to take a feature-based approach

records.content:
Rather than feeding the raw 32-slot allocation time series to the neural network (which caused training instability due to sparsity, variable sequence lengths, and the curse of dimensionality), we extract a fixed-size vector of 40 behavioral summary statistics per session. This approach eliminates variable-length padding issues, replaces sparse integer inputs with continuous, bounded features, ensures identical feature computation for simulated and real participant data (no domain shift) and thus reduces the neural network’s task from “learn to extract behavioral patterns from raw sequences” to “learn to map extracted patterns to parameters” - which also best aligns with the core research focus.

Furthermore, this approach simplifies the integration of multimodal data while maintaining the same network architecture; and - even more interestingly - it potentially allows for generalization to various tasks rather than being limited to a single paradigm.

All 40 features satisfy the following requirements (to ensure traceability and computational stability):

Continuous and bounded: No discrete or unbounded values that could destabilize training

Deterministic: Given the same allocation sequence \{\mathbf{g}_t\} and schedule, the features are identical

Domain-invariant: The same extraction code operates on both simulated agent output and real participant data (to avoid preprocessing differences that could create domain shift)

Information-preserving: Each feature is designed to capture a specific aspect of behavior that distinguishes between parameter values, based on prior sensitivity analysis (see The inference agent, associated behavioral features in the parameter definition section)

The 40 specified features are organized into 8 groups, each targeting different aspects of behavior:

On a behavioral level, this set of features reflect how participants react to oddballs. Consequently, they reflect belief stability, how well participants identified the underlying latent structures (if a participant knows, that an environmental transition occures all ~20 trials, this participant would learn to ignore single oddball events within episodes without getting highly irritated - oddballs are not longer unexpected and suprising, they do not longer foster faster belief updating, e.g. oddball-sensitivity). records.content:
Feature

Description

defense_rate_slope

Linear slope of defense rate across episodes

angular_error_slope

Linear slope of angular error across episodes

odd_even_episode_diff

Defense rate difference between odd and even episodes

1. mean_net_entropy — Mean Shannon entropy (base 2) of the net allocation over all trials: \bar{H} = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^T H(\mathbf{g}_t), where H(\mathbf{g}) = -\sum_s p_s \log_2 p_s and p_s = g_s / \sum_j g_j.

2. std_net_entropy — Standard deviation of per-trial entropy: \text{SD}(H(\mathbf{g}_1), \ldots, H(\mathbf{g}_T)).

3. mean_slots_occupied — Mean number of slots with \geq 1 net: \frac{1}{T} \sum_t |\{s : g_{t,s} > 0\}|.

4. mean_max_nets_slot — Mean maximum stacking depth: \frac{1}{T} \sum_t \max_s g_{t,s}.

5. frac_stacking — Fraction of trials where any slot has \geq 2 nets: \frac{1}{T} \sum_t \mathbb{I}[\max_s g_{t,s} \geq 2].

6. mean_circ_variance — Mean circular variance of the allocation: \bar{V} = \frac{1}{T} \sum_t (1 - R_t), where R_t = \|\sum_s p_s e^{i\phi_s}\| is the resultant length.

7. entropy_change_halves — Difference in mean entropy between the second and first halves of the session: \bar{H}_{t > T/2} - \bar{H}_{t \leq T/2}.

8. mean_angular_error — Mean unsigned angular distance (degrees) between the allocation circular mean and the actual outcome: \frac{1}{|\mathcal{O}|} \sum_{t \in \mathcal{O}} |\angle(\omega_t - \bar{\phi}_t^g)|, where \mathcal{O} is the set of outcome trials and \bar{\phi}_t^g is the circular mean of \mathbf{g}_t.

9. std_angular_error — Standard deviation of per-trial angular errors.

10. mean_defense_rate — Fraction of outcome trials where the attacked slot has \geq 1 net: \frac{1}{|\mathcal{O}|} \sum_{t \in \mathcal{O}} \mathbb{I}[g_{t, s(\omega_t)} > 0], where s(\omega) = \text{round}(\omega \cdot S / 2\pi) \mod S.

11. defense_rate_improvement — Defense rate in the last 3 episodes minus the first 3 episodes.

12. mean_tracking_error — Mean angular distance (degrees) between the allocation center on trial t and the previous outcome \omega_{t-1}. Measures how closely the agent tracks the most recent observation.

13. oddball_entropy_spike — Mean entropy on trial t+1 after an oddball, minus mean entropy on regular trials.

14. oddball_angular_shift — Mean angular shift (degrees) of allocation center from trial t-1 to t+1 around oddball trials.

15. oddball_angular_shift_regular — Same metric computed around non-oddball outcome trials (baseline).

16. oddball_shift_ratio — Feature 14 divided by Feature 15. Values > 1 indicate the agent reacts more strongly to oddballs than to regular outcomes.

17. oddball_recovery_speed — Mean angular distance of allocation center on trials t+1, t+2, t+3 from the pre-oddball position (t-1), averaged across oddballs.

18. frac_oddball_followed — Fraction of oddballs where the allocation on t+1 is closer to the oddball direction \omega_t than the allocation on t-1 was.

19–21. transition_angular_error_t1/t3/t5 — Mean angular error on trials 1, 3, and 5 after episode transitions (z-state switches).

22. transition_recovery_slope — Linear regression slope of angular error over trials 1–5 after transitions. Negative slope = error decreasing = fast recovery.

23. transition_entropy_peak — Maximum entropy in the first 3 post-transition trials.

24. transition_entropy_recovery — Number of trials until entropy returns to baseline (\leq \bar{H} + 0.5 \cdot \text{SD}(H)) after a transition.

25. mean_angular_error_late_episode — Mean angular error on trials 6+ within each episode (baseline accuracy after initial learning).

26. transition_recovery_improvement — Linear slope of recovery speeds across the 8 transitions. Positive = recovery gets faster over the session (meta-learning).

27. cue_offset_learned — Mean angular offset (degrees) between allocation centers for Cue A vs. Cue B, computed per episode and averaged. True value: 70°.

28. cue_offset_std — Standard deviation of per-episode cue offsets.

29. cue_defense_rate_diff — Mean absolute difference in defense rate between Cue A and Cue B trials, per episode.

30. cue_entropy_diff — Mean absolute difference in entropy between Cue A and Cue B trials, per episode.

31. mean_trial_shift — Mean angular shift (degrees) of allocation center between consecutive trials.

32. std_trial_shift — Standard deviation of trial-to-trial shifts.

33. frac_large_shifts — Fraction of consecutive trial pairs with > 45° shift (proxy for perceived state switches).

34. autocorr_lag1 — Circular autocorrelation at lag 1: \frac{1}{T-1} \sum_t \cos(\bar{\phi}_{t+1}^g - \bar{\phi}_t^g). High values = stable allocation direction.

35. autocorr_lag3 — Same at lag 3 (captures stability across cue alternations).

36. omission_entropy_change — Mean entropy change from trial t-1 to t+1 around omission trials.

37. omission_shift — Mean angular shift of allocation center from t-1 to t+1 around omissions. Should be near zero for a normative agent (no information received on omission trials).

38. defense_rate_slope — Linear regression slope of per-episode defense rate over the 9 episodes.

39. angular_error_slope — Linear regression slope of per-episode angular error.

40. odd_even_episode_diff — Absolute difference in defense rate between odd-numbered and even-numbered episodes. Captures asymmetry in learning across alternating episodes.

records.content:
This chapter describes the computational modeling pipeline used to estimate participant-specific cognitive parameters from behavioral data. The pipeline combines three components:

The inference agent - a parametric belief-update model that maps cognitive parameters to observable behavior

A feature extraction module - deterministic extraction of 40 behavioral summary statistics from net allocations

An amortized Bayesian inference network - a neural density estimator (BayesFlow) that inverts the agent model, mapping observed behavioral features to posterior distributions over cognitive parameters

The key advantage of this methodological approach is that inference is amortized. Specificially, a neural network is trained once on a large corpus of simulated agent–environment interactions, after which posterior estimation for any real participant takes approximately 3 seconds. This enables full Bayesian uncertainty quantification without the computational cost of traditional MCMC methods.

Further information about Amortized Bayesian Inference and BayesFlow 2.0 can be found here (insert Radev et al., 2023 and website link)

records.content:
Participants in the TAMBI mosquito defense task face a complex learning problem: they must infer hidden environmental regularities - latent state transitions, cue-dependent directional offsets, and the distinction between noise and genuine change - from noisy, intermittent outcome feedback. Not all participants approach this problem in the same way. Some may engage in sophisticated latent structure inference, tracking hidden states and attributing surprises to their appropriate sources. Others may rely on simpler strategies: following the most recent outcome direction, or learning the association between cue colours and attack directions without ever building a model of the latent state dynamics. A small number may not engage meaningfully at all.

Before we can interpret the cognitive parameters estimated by our inference agent (

Amortized Bayesian Inference), we need to establish which type of learning strategy each participant is using. This is essential for three reasons:

Validity of parameter estimates. The four cognitive parameters (precision weighting, noise reactivity, belief momentum, perceived volatility) are defined within a latent-structure-learning agent. Estimating these parameters for a participant who is not performing structure learning would be scientifically inappropriate (analogous to fitting a linear regression model to non-linear data)

Participant exclusion. Participants who clicked randomly or without meaningful engagement should be identified and excluded from subsequent analyses. Here, model comparison provides an objective, data-driven exclusion criterion.

Characterisation of the learning strategy spectrum. The distribution of winning models across participants provides insight into the prevalence of different learning strategies in the sample, and can be related to instruction condition and trait-level measures.

hierarchy.lvl1: Model Comparison: Classifying Learning Strategies in the TAMBI Task
Model Comparison: Classifying Learning Strategies in the TAMBI Task

records.content:
We employ BayesFlow’s ModelComparisonApproximator (Radev et al., 2023; Schmitt et al., 2023) to perform amortized Bayesian model comparison. The approach follows the same simulation-based inference logic as the parameter estimation pipeline: a neural classifier is trained on a large corpus of simulated behavioral data (generated from all candidate models), and then applied to real participant data to estimate posterior model probabilities.

The key advantage over traditional model comparison methods (e.g., BIC, AIC, Bayes factors via bridge sampling) is that amortized model comparison:

Operates on the same 40 behavioral summary features used for parameter estimation (ensuring consistency)

Provides well-calibrated posterior probabilities over models (not just point estimates or ranking)

Requires no per-participant optimization or sampling at inference time (~1 ms per participant)

Can be validated via confusion matrices, calibration curves, and model recovery analyses before application to real data

Model Comparison: Classifying Learning Strategies in the TAMBI Task

records.content:
Rather than comparing a large number of models that differ in subtle ways, we define a compact set of four models that span the qualitative spectrum from disengaged behavior to full latent structure inference. This design reflects a principled ablation logic: each successive model adds a qualitatively distinct cognitive capability.



Figure 1:The four models.

The rationale for limiting the set to four models is twofold. First, the three cognitive transitions (no learning → outcome tracking → cue association → structure inference) capture the most important qualitative distinctions in learning strategy. Second, model variants that differ only in continuous parameter settings within the structure-learning agent (e.g. with or without oddball attribution, with fixed or free volatility) are better handled by the parameter estimation pipeline, where these differences manifest as different parameter values rather than as discrete model classes. Maintaining them as separate models would constitute “double dipping” - testing the same cognitive dimension at both the model and parameter level.

records.content:
Purpose: Participant exclusion criterion. Identifies data that are indistinguishable from random allocation.

Mechanism: On each trial, 7 nets are placed by drawing 7 slot indices uniformly at random (with replacement) from the 32 available slots. This produces allocations where each slot has approximately equal probability of receiving a net, with stochastic variation in the number of nets per slot due to the multinomial sampling.

Free parameters: None.

Behavioral signature: High entropy, low defense rate, zero autocorrelation, no systematic response to oddballs or transitions.

records.content:
Purpose: Tests whether behavior can be explained by simple exponential moving average (EMA) tracking of the most recent outcome direction, without any cue differentiation or latent state inference.

Mechanism: The agent maintains a single direction estimate \hat{\mu} that is updated after each observed outcome \omega_t via a circular EMA:\hat{\mu} \leftarrow \left(\hat{\mu} + \alpha \cdot \angle(\omega_t - \hat{\mu})\right) \mod 2\pi

where \alpha is the learning rate and \angle(\cdot) denotes the signed angular distance in [-\pi, \pi]. The agent does not distinguish between cue colours: outcomes from both Cue A and Cue B update the same direction estimate \hat{\mu}. Net allocation uses the same power-sharpening mechanism as the full agent:g_s = \text{round}\left(\frac{[\text{vM}(\phi_s; \hat{\mu}, \kappa_{\text{eff}})]^{1+\text{pw}}}{\sum_j [\text{vM}(\phi_j; \hat{\mu}, \kappa_{\text{eff}})]^{1+\text{pw}}} \cdot 7\right)

where \kappa_{\text{eff}} = \kappa_{\text{fixed}} \cdot \text{pw}.

Free parameters:

Parameter

Range

Description

precision_weighting

[0.1, 5.0]

Controls allocation concentration (same as M_STRUCT)

\alpha

[0.01, 0.5]

EMA learning rate

What this model cannot capture: Cue-specific behaviour (different placements for Cue A vs. Cue B), latent state transitions (sudden direction changes), or oddball attribution.

records.content:
Purpose: Tests whether behaviour can be explained by trial-by-trial tracking with learned cue-colour associations. This represents a form of associative learning that does not require inferring hidden states.

Mechanism: Identical to M_TRACK_SIMPLE, but with the addition of a cue-specific angular offset \hat{\delta}_B that is learned online:

For Cue A: prediction at \hat{\mu}_A

For Cue B: prediction at (\hat{\mu}_A + \hat{\delta}_B) \mod 2\pi

When a Cue B outcome is observed, both the base direction \hat{\mu}_A and the offset \hat{\delta}_B are updated with different learning rates:\hat{\delta}_B \leftarrow (\hat{\delta}_B + \alpha \cdot 0.3 \cdot \text{PE}_B) \mod 2\pi\hat{\mu}_A \leftarrow (\hat{\mu}_A + \alpha \cdot 0.7 \cdot \text{PE}_B) \mod 2\pi

where \text{PE}_B = \angle(\omega_t - [\hat{\mu}_A + \hat{\delta}_B]).

Free parameters: Same as M_TRACK_SIMPLE (precision_weighting, \alpha). The offset \hat{\delta}_B is a learned internal state, not a prior parameter.

What this model adds over M_TRACK_SIMPLE: The ability to place nets at different angular positions depending on the cue colour - a directly observable regularity in the task.

What this model cannot capture: Latent state transitions (the agent has no concept of alternating hidden states), oddball attribution (all surprises are treated as signal), or episode-boundary adaptation.

records.content:
Purpose: The primary model. Tests whether behaviour reflects latent structure inference - tracking hidden states, attributing surprises to oddball noise versus genuine change, and adapting beliefs after state transitions.

Mechanism: The full reduced-form inference agent as described in the Parameter Estimation chapter. Implements a two-state Hidden Markov Model with cue-dependent offsets, oddball mixture observation model, and belief momentum.

Free parameters: precision_weighting, noise_reactivity, belief_momentum, h_switch (4 parameters total). See the Parameter Estimation chapter for complete specification.

What this model adds over M_TRACK_CUE: The concept of latent states (z \in \{0,1\}) that transition with probability h, producing systematic direction changes that the agent can anticipate. The oddball mixture that allows the agent to separate noise from signal. The belief momentum that captures individual differences in adaptation speed.

records.content:
All four models produce integer net allocations \mathbf{g}_t \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}^{32} on every trial. These allocations are processed through the same 40-feature extraction pipeline used for parameter estimation (see Appendix A of the 

Feature Extraction chapter). This ensures:

No domain shift: The classifier sees the same feature representation for training (simulated data from all four models) and inference (real participant data).

Consistency with parameter estimation: A participant classified as M_STRUCT by the model comparison will have their subsequent parameter estimates computed from the same features.

Interpretability: The features that drive classification can be inspected directly (e.g. “M_RANDOM is distinguished from M_TRACK_SIMPLE primarily by low autocorrelation and high entropy”).

records.content:
The model comparison uses BayesFlow 2.0’s ModelComparisonApproximator, which implements amortized Bayesian model comparison via a neural classifier trained on simulated data from all candidate models.

The architecture consists of:

Adapter: Renames the model index to inference_variables, applies one-hot encoding (\mathbb{R}^1 \to \mathbb{R}^4), and converts to float32.

Summary Network: A DeepSet (Zaheer et al., 2017) with summary_dim=8 and depth=1 processes the feature vector (reshaped to a 1-element set). The DeepSet maps the 40-dimensional feature vector to an 8-dimensional embedding.

Classifier Network: An MLP with two hidden layers of 64 units each (SiLU activation, no dropout) maps the 8-dimensional embedding to 4 logits, which are passed through a softmax to produce posterior model probabilities.

The total number of trainable parameters is approximately 8,000 (which is quite small - this is intentional and allows to prevent overfitting, given the relatively low complexity of the 4-class classification problem).

records.content:
Data generation. N = 40{,}000 simulations are generated in parallel (10,000 per model, balanced). For each simulation:

A model is selected uniformly at random from {M_RANDOM, M_TRACK_SIMPLE, M_TRACK_CUE, M_STRUCT}

Parameters are drawn from the model’s prior

A trial schedule is randomly selected from the 20 available schedules

The model simulates the full session

The 40-feature vector is extracted

Class balance is maintained by uniform model sampling during generation.

Training. The classifier is trained via cross-entropy loss for 200 epochs with:

Batch size: 512

Cosine learning rate decay: 10^{-4} \to 10^{-6}

Train/validation split: 90%/10%

No early stopping (the small network converges quickly and does not overfit)

Loss function. The standard categorical cross-entropy:\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{m=1}^{4} y_{i,m} \log \hat{p}_{i,m}

where y_{i,m} is the one-hot encoded true model index and \hat{p}_{i,m} is the predicted probability for model m.

records.content: Model comparison validation is more extensive than parameter recovery validation, because misclassification has direct consequences for participant inclusion/exclusion and for the interpretation of subsequent parameter estimates. We perform five complementary validation analyses.

records.content:
A balanced test set (1,000 simulations per model = 4,000 total) is generated from fresh parameter draws and schedules not used in training. For each simulation, the classifier predicts the model with highest posterior probability. The confusion matrix shows, for each true model, the fraction of simulations classified as each predicted model.

Target: Diagonal entries > 0.80 for all models. Off-diagonal entries should be small and concentrated on “adjacent” models in the ablation hierarchy (e.g. some M_TRACK_CUE simulations misclassified as M_TRACK_SIMPLE, but very few M_RANDOM simulations misclassified as M_STRUCT).

Results:



Figure 2:The confusion matrix shows r = 1.0, which corresponds to 100% accuracy.

records.content:
For each model, we compute a reliability diagram, showing the relationship between the classifier’s predicted probability (confidence) and the empirical frequency of correct classification. A well-calibrated classifier has predicted probability equal to empirical accuracy across all confidence levels.

The Expected Calibration Error (ECE) quantifies the deviation from perfect calibration:\text{ECE} = \sum_{b=1}^{B} \frac{n_b}{N} \left| \text{acc}(b) - \text{conf}(b) \right|

where the confidence range [0, 1] is partitioned into B = 10 bins, n_b is the number of samples in bin b, and \text{acc}(b) and \text{conf}(b) are the mean accuracy and confidence within that bin.

Target: ECE < 0.05 for all models (i.e., predicted probabilities are reliable).

records.content:
For each model: when data were generated from that model, what is the distribution of posterior probability assigned to the true model? This is the model-comparison analogue of Simulation-Based Calibration (Talts et al., 2018): if the classifier is well-calibrated, the distribution of p(\text{true model} \mid \text{data}) should be concentrated near 1.0 for unambiguous cases and spread toward 0.5 for cases where two models produce similar behaviour (e.g., M_TRACK_SIMPLE with high learning rate and M_TRACK_CUE with near-zero \hat{\delta}_B).

We report:

Mean p(\text{true}): The average posterior probability assigned to the generating model. Higher = better discrimination.

Fraction correct (p > 0.5): How often the true model has the highest probability. This equals the per-model accuracy from the confusion matrix.

Fraction confident (p > 0.8): How often the classifier is highly confident about the correct model. This measures the strength of the behavioral signatures.

records.content:
For M_STRUCT specifically we check whether the classifier’s accuracy depend on the parameter values used to generate the data. This is central to evaluate whether some parameter regions produce behaviour that is indistinguishable from simpler models (and thus inducing identifyability issues). For example, M_STRUCT with very low noise reactivity and high belief momentum might resemble M_TRACK_CUE, because the agent rarely switches states. Conversely, M_STRUCT with very high h_switch might produce behaviour so variable that it resembles M_RANDOM.

We partition the parameter space into quintiles for each of the four M_STRUCT parameters and compute classification accuracy within each quintile. This identifies potential “blind spots” in the classification and informs the interpretation of boundary cases in real participant data.

Results: However, this is not the case for our models as this figure shows.

Results:



Figure 5:Literally perfect accuracy across parameter values.

records.content: A participant is flagged for exclusion if p(\text{M\_RANDOM}) > p(m) for all other models m (i.e. the random model has the highest posterior probability). That means, a participant is only excluded when their behaviour is genuinely better described by random allocation than by any form of directed learning.

records.content:
The model comparison and parameter estimation pipelines are designed to be used sequentially:

Model comparison classifies each participant’s learning strategy

Participants classified as M_RANDOM are excluded

Participants classified as M_STRUCT have their cognitive parameters estimated via the parameter estimation pipeline

Participants classified as M_TRACK_SIMPLE or M_TRACK_CUE are reported as using simpler strategies; their parameter estimates (if computed) should be interpreted with caution, as the structure-learning parameters (noise_reactivity, belief_momentum, h_switch) are not meaningful for agents that do not perform structure learning

This sequential design ensures that cognitive parameters are only interpreted for participants whose behaviour is consistent with the generating model of those parameters.

records.content:
The three instruction conditions create different expectations about the model comparison results:

Condition 1 (no rules): Participants must discover all regularities through experience. We expect the full spectrum, including some M_TRACK_SIMPLE (slow learners), some M_TRACK_CUE (learn cue association but not state transitions), and some M_STRUCT (full structure learning).

Condition 2 (rules mentioned): Participants are told that cue colours are associated with different directions but not given specific values. We expect fewer M_TRACK_SIMPLE (the cue association is primed) and more M_TRACK_CUE or M_STRUCT.

Condition 3 (rules explained): Participants are given the exact cue offset (70°) and state rotation (120°). We expect predominantly M_STRUCT, because the explicit rule knowledge should enable rapid structure learning.

The model comparison thus provides a manipulation check (does the instruction condition shift participants toward more sophisticated learning strategies, as intended?)

records.content:
Radev, S. T., Schmitt, M., Schumacher, L., Elsemüller, L., Pratz, V., Schälte, Y., ... & Bürkner, P. C. (2023). BayesFlow: Amortized Bayesian workflows with neural networks. Journal of Open Source Software.

Schmitt, M., Bürkner, P. C., Köthe, U., & Radev, S. T. (2023). Detecting model misspecification in amortized Bayesian inference with neural networks. GCPR 2023.

Talts, S., Betancourt, M., Simpson, D., Vehtari, A., & Gelman, A. (2018). Validating Bayesian inference algorithms with simulation-based calibration. arXiv preprint arXiv:1804.06788.

Zaheer, M., Kottur, S., Ravanbakhsh, S., Poczos, B., Salakhutdinov, R. R., & Smola, A. J. (2017). Deep sets. NeurIPS 2017.

The TAMBI task generates rich trial-by-trial behavioral data (32-slot net allocations over ~180 trials). To connect this behavior to latent cognitive processes, we require a generative model that:

Retains the scientific structure of the task (2-state HMM, cue offset, oddball mixture, von-Mises observation model)

Has parameters that directly and transparently affect behavioral output

Produces behavioral patterns that match the range observed in human participants

Is computationally efficient enough to generate the ~100,000 simulations required for amortized inference training

The reduced-form agent satisfies all four requirements. It replaces the particle filter approximation (used in the simulation models for model comparison) with analytically tractable update rules, ensuring that each parameter has a direct, measurable effect on the allocation output without being “washed out” by particle dynamics. Additionally, the simplified action selection allows to focus on the underlying process of inference and belief updating - implementing inference with free parameters for both at a time would cause critical ambiguity and uncontrolable parameter-interaction effects in the inference process.

The agent maintains point estimates of the following latent state variables, updated on each trial:

State Variable

Symbol

Description

Cue A direction

\mu_A

Believed attack direction for Cue A under the current z-state

Cue offset

\delta_B

Angular offset between Cue A and Cue B directions

State rotation

\Delta

Angular rotation between z-states (~120°)

z-belief

p(z_t = 1)

Soft belief over which latent context state is active

Two structural parameters are fixed across all participants:

Fixed Parameter

Value

Justification

\kappa_{\text{fixed}}

5.0

Calibrated so that precision_weighting produces the full range of VP-observed allocation patterns (1–7 occupied slots, with realistic stacking)

\rho_{\text{fixed}}

0.10

Baseline oddball probability matching the TAMBI design (~10% oddball rate)

Fixing \kappa and \rho ensures that the four free parameters have strictly monotone, uncompensated effects on behavior throughout the entire session. Online learning of \kappa and \rho was found to create compensatory dynamics that reduced parameter identifiability. Importantly, the update-step likelihoods (Section xxx) use \kappa_{\text{fixed}} directly, while the predictive distribution (Section xxx) uses \kappa_{\text{eff}} = \kappa_{\text{fixed}} \cdot \text{pw}. This prevents a confound where precision weighting would simultaneously make the agent more confident in its predictions and more responsive to prediction errors.
The agent has four free parameters estimated per participant via BayesFlow:

\text{pw} \in [0.1, 5.0]

Controls how tightly the net allocation follows the agent’s learned direction. Mechanically, precision weighting controls three aspects of the agent’s behavior:

Predictive concentration:
\kappa_{\text{eff}} = \kappa_{\text{fixed}} \cdot \text{pw}


This scales the von Mises concentration used in the predictive distribution, determining how sharply the predicted attack distribution peaks around the believed direction.

Allocation sharpening:
\alpha = 1 + \text{pw}


The predictive slot probabilities \pi_s are raised to the power \alpha before integer rounding, controlling how concentrated the net placement is.

Direction learning rate:
\eta_\mu = 0.05 \cdot \text{pw}


This scales the prediction-error-driven update of \hat{\mu}_A, determining how quickly the agent adjusts its direction estimate after each observation.

These three mechanisms are intentionally coupled to represent a certain mechanistic behavioral pattern. An agent with high precision weighting believes outcomes are precise (narrow predictive distribution), places nets precisely (high sharpening), and updates its direction estimate quickly (high learning rate). This coherence reflects the theoretical construct of precision weighting in predictive processing (Friston, 2008; Mathys et al., 2014).

Associated behavioral features: Net entropy (bits), number of occupied slots, stacking depth.

Identifiability: r = 0.998 (parameter recovery), primary behavioral signature: net entropy, slots occupied, stacking fraction.

\text{nr} \in [0.1, 3.0]

Controls how the agent attributes surprising observations to noise (oddballs) versus genuine environmental change (state switches). (This is inspired by the computational formalization of the Piray & Daw (2021) “broken unpredictability estimator” hypothesis)

The effective oddball rate \rho_{\text{eff}} = \rho_{\text{fixed}} \cdot \text{nr} modulates the likelihood mixture:L(z) = (1 - \rho_{\text{eff}}) \cdot f_{\text{vM}}(\omega_t; \mu_z, \kappa) + \rho_{\text{eff}} \cdot f_{\text{vM}}(\omega_t; \mu_z + \Delta, \kappa)

That way, higher \rho_{\text{eff}} increases the weight of the oddball component in the marginal likelihood, reducing the evidence for state change when a surprising outcome is observed.

Additionally, noise reactivity gates the direction update via the oddball responsibility:\gamma = \frac{\rho_{\text{eff}} \cdot L_{\text{odd}}}{(1 - \rho_{\text{eff}}) \cdot L_{\text{reg}} + \rho_{\text{eff}} \cdot L_{\text{odd}}}

The oddball responsibility \gamma_t determines how much the direction update is dampened after a surprising observation. The direction update is scaled by (1 - \gamma). That means, when noise reactivity is high, \rho_{\text{eff}} is high, \gamma is high for surprising observations, and the update is dampened (the agent treats the surprise as noise and shows weaker learning from this trial). When noise reactivity is low, \rho_{\text{eff}} is low, \gamma is low, and the agent fully updates its direction estimate (treating the surprise as a real signal). Low noise reactivity (\text{nr} < 1) corresponds to an agent that underestimates the oddball rate, causing unexpected outcomes to be interpreted as state changes rather than random fluctuations, which produces a behavioral pattern that is characterized by excessive belief updating, abrupt state switches, and elevated perceived volatility. The complementary case (high noise reactivity (\text{nr} > 1)) describes an agent that over-attributes surprising outcomes to noise, potentially missing genuine state changes.

(My idea was that this parameter could seperate anxiety and depression in a reasonable manner)

Associated behavioral features: Oddball shift ratio, fraction of oddballs followed, oddball recovery speed.

Identifiability: r = 0.948, primary behavioral signature: oddball shift ratio, fraction of oddballs followed, oddball recovery speed.

\text{bm} \in [0.0, 0.95]

Controls resistance to z-state belief revision. After computing the standard Bayesian posterior p(z=1 \mid \text{data})_{\text{raw}}, the agent applies exponential smoothing:p(z=1)_t = (1 - \text{bm}) \cdot p(z=1 \mid \text{data})_{\text{raw}} + \text{bm} \cdot p(z=1)_{t-1}

At \text{bm} = 0, the agent is a perfect Bayesian updater (instantaneous belief revision). At \text{bm} = 0.9, only 10% of the new evidence is incorporated per trial, requiring ~10 trials to approach the new posterior. This parameter captures individual differences in cognitive flexibility versus rigidity, and may relate to intolerance of uncertainty or perseverative tendencies. It is distinct from noise reactivity in that it applies to all belief updates (including those following genuine state changes), not only to oddball attribution.

Note on how to distinguish belief momentum from h_switch: Both parameters affect the dynamics of p(z = 1) over time. However, they operate at different stages of the update. h (perceived volatility) acts in the prediction step, pulling p(z = 1) toward 0.5 before each observation, which creates a baseline drift that is constant across trials. \text{bm} (belief momentum), on the other hand, acts in the update step, dampening the posterior revision after each observation, which creates sluggish transitions that are most visible around episode boundaries.

Associated behavioral features: Transition recovery slope, angular error in early post-switch trials, allocation autocorrelation.

Identifiability: r = 0.987, primary behavioral signature: transition recovery slope, angular error in first trials after episode transitions, allocation autocorrelation.

h \in [0.005, 0.20]

This is the HMM transition probability used in the prediction step (the agent’s prior belief about how frequently the latent state switches):p_t^{\text{pred}}(z = 1) = p_{t-1}(z = 1) \cdot (1 - h) + [1 - p_{t-1}(z = 1)] \cdot h

Higher h means the agent expects more frequent state switches, which manifests as a persistent pull of p(z = 1) toward 0.5 (maximum uncertainty). Agents with high h may hedge between both possible directions, placing nets at two locations simultaneously.

The true transition rate in the TAMBI task is h_{\text{true}} = 1/20 = 0.05 (one switch per ~20 trials). Participants who over-estimate h perceive the environment as more volatile than it is.

Associated behavioral features: Fraction of large allocation shifts, mean trial-to-trial angular shift, defense rate trajectory across episodes.

Identifiability: r = 0.954, primary behavioral signature: fraction of large allocation shifts, mean trial-to-trial shift, defense rate slope across episodes.

Given the current state estimates, the agent computes the predictive slot probabilities by marginalizing over the latent state z:\pi_s = \sum_{z \in \{0,1\}} p^{\text{pred}}(z) \left[ (1 - \rho_{\text{eff}}) \cdot \text{vM}(\phi_s; \hat{\mu}_{c,z}, \kappa_{\text{eff}}) + \rho_{\text{eff}} \cdot \text{vM}(\phi_s; \hat{\mu}_{c,z} + \hat{\Delta}, \kappa_{\text{eff}}) \right]

where \text{vM}(\phi; \mu, \kappa) \propto \exp(\kappa \cos(\phi - \mu)) and the summation is over the S = 32 slot angles \phi_s. The slot probabilities are normalized: \pi_s \leftarrow \pi_s / \sum_j \pi_j.

This predictive distribution incorporates:

Uncertainty over the latent state (via p^{\text{pred}}(z))

Uncertainty about whether the next trial will be an oddball (via \rho_{\text{eff}})

The agent’s confidence in its direction estimate (via \kappa_{\text{eff}})

The agent converts its predictive distribution to a net allocation via a two-step process:

Sharpening: The predictive probability per slot \pi_s is raised to the power \alpha = 1 + \text{pw}: \tilde{\pi}_s = \pi_s^\alpha / \sum_j \pi_j^\alpha

Integer rounding: The continuous allocation \tilde{\pi}_s \cdot 7 is rounded to integers via largest-remainder rounding, ensuring the total equals 7.

This produces realistic stacking patterns: at \text{pw} = 1.5, the agent places nets in ~5 slots with pattern [2,2,1,1,1]; at \text{pw} = 3.0, in ~3 slots with pattern [3,2,2]; at \text{pw} = 5.0, in ~2 slots with pattern [6,1]. These patterns match the range observed in human participants.

On omission trials (no attack outcome), only the HMM prediction is applied:p_t(z = 1) \leftarrow p_t^{\text{pred}}(z = 1)

No direction or state updates occur. The oddball gate defaults to \gamma_t = 0 (gate = 1), and the prediction error is undefined.

hierarchy.lvl3: Latent State Update with Belief Momentum
Latent State Update with Belief Momentum

records.content:
The dominant state is \hat{z}_t = \mathbb{I}[p_t(z = 1) > 0.5]. The prediction error is:\text{PE}_t = \angle(\omega_t - \hat{\mu}_{c_t, \hat{z}_t})

where \angle(\cdot) denotes the signed angular distance in [-\pi, \pi].

The oddball responsibility under the dominant state is:\gamma_t = \frac{\rho_{\text{eff}} \cdot L_{\text{odd}}^{(\hat{z}_t)}}{(1 - \rho_{\text{eff}}) \cdot L_{\text{reg}}^{(\hat{z}_t)} + \rho_{\text{eff}} \cdot L_{\text{odd}}^{(\hat{z}_t)}}

The gating factor is g_t = 1 - \gamma_t. When g_t \approx 1 (low oddball responsibility), the observation is treated as regular and the full prediction error drives the update. When g_t \approx 0 (high oddball responsibility), the observation is likely an oddball and the direction update is suppressed.

For Cue A observations:\hat{\mu}_A \leftarrow (\hat{\mu}_A + \eta_\mu \cdot g_t \cdot \text{PE}_t) \mod 2\pi

For Cue B observations:\hat{\delta}_B \leftarrow (\hat{\delta}_B + \eta_\mu \cdot g_t \cdot \text{PE}_B \cdot 0.5) \mod 2\pi

\hat{\mu}_A \leftarrow (\hat{\mu}_A + \eta_\mu \cdot g_t \cdot \text{PE}_B \cdot 0.3) \mod 2\pi

where \text{PE}_B = \angle(\omega_t - [\hat{\mu}_A + \hat{z}_t \cdot \hat{\Delta} + \hat{\delta}_B]) and \eta_\mu = 0.05 \cdot \text{pw}.

The asymmetric learning rates for Cue B (0.5 for \hat{\delta}_B, 0.3 for \hat{\mu}_A) reflect the fact that Cue B observations provide more direct evidence about the cue offset than about the base direction.

The state rotation \hat{\Delta} is updated only when the agent is uncertain about the current state (i.e., near state transitions):z_{\text{unc}} = 1 - |2 \cdot p_t(z = 1) - 1|\hat{\Delta} \leftarrow (\hat{\Delta} + 0.01 \cdot z_{\text{unc}} \cdot \angle(\omega_t - \hat{\mu}_{c_t, 1 - \hat{z}_t})) \mod 2\pi

The small learning rate (0.01) and the uncertainty gating ensure that \hat{\Delta} is updated primarily during state transitions, when the agent has access to observations from both states in close temporal proximity.

The agent produces two types of output per trial:

Net allocation \mathbf{g}_t \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}^{32}: The observable behavioral response, identical in format to participant data.

Latent summary features (5 per trial):

\sin(\bar{\phi}_t), \cos(\bar{\phi}_t): Circular mean direction of the predictive distribution (before action selection)

V_t^{\text{circ}}: Circular variance of the predictive distribution (= 1 - R, where R is the resultant length)

p_t(z = 1): State belief before the trial’s update

g_t: Oddball gate value (1 - \gamma_t)
These latent features are used during training to provide the neural network with rich temporal information about the agent’s internal dynamics. For participant data inference, a proxy forward pass with default parameters generates these features (see Section xxx).

9.1

This chapter documents the design decisions that apply across the behavioral data analysis pipeline. The model chapters 

Primary GLMMs, 

Temporal Dynamics, and 

Factor Structure cross-reference back to the corresponding section here.

hierarchy.lvl2: Treatment of the ordered condition factor
Treatment of the ordered condition factor

records.content:
The instruction-condition factor takes three values \{1, 2, 3\} corresponding to {basic, rules-mentioned, rules-explained}, see 

Instruction Conditions. The values are theoretically ordered along a single epistemic-uncertainty axis: each step from condition 1 to 3 reduces explicit information about the task structure. We encode this via orthogonal polynomial contrasts (equivalent to R’s contr.poly(3)):\text{condition\_L}_{1,2,3} = \big\{-\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, 0, +\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\big\} \approx \{-0.707, 0, +0.707\},\text{condition\_Q}_{1,2,3} = \big\{+\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{6}}, -\tfrac{2}{\sqrt{6}}, +\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{6}}\big\} \approx \{+0.408, -0.816, +0.408\}.

The two contrasts are orthogonal (\sum_c L_c \cdot Q_c = 0) and unit-norm (\sum_c L_c^2 = \sum_c Q_c^2 = 1).

hierarchy.lvl2: Treatment of the ordered condition factor
Treatment of the ordered condition factor

records.content:
Linear contrast condition_L captures the monotonic effect of moving from condition 1 to condition 3. A coefficient of \beta_L on this contrast corresponds to a per-unit-contrast effect; the predicted difference between condition 1 and condition 3 on the response scale is \sqrt{2} \cdot \beta_L \approx 1.414 \cdot \beta_L.

Quadratic contrast condition_Q captures non-monotonicity: a significant condition_Q indicates that condition 2’s mean is meaningfully different from a linear interpolation between conditions 1 and 3. This is theoretically secondary, but it serves as control, as it absorbs any non-monotonic pattern (e.g. condition 2 being systematically different because vague hints prime attention without resolving uncertainty), preventing such patterns from leaking into the linear contrast estimate.

hierarchy.lvl3: How this design translates to the model implementations
hierarchy.lvl2: Treatment of the ordered condition factor

hierarchy.lvl3: How this design translates to the model implementations
hierarchy.lvl2: Treatment of the ordered condition factor

records.content: Every fixed-effect specification involving condition contains two condition terms (condition_L, condition_Q), and every condition-by-X interaction expands to two interactions (condition_L : X, condition_Q : X). The headline test is on condition_L : X; the test on condition_Q : X is reported as a secondary check.

Episodes are 16–24 trials long by design (see 

The Task Structure). This jitter creates three distinct statistical risks if not handled deliberately.

hierarchy.lvl4: Risk 1: Imbalanced cells from raw trial_in_episode
Risk 1: Imbalanced cells from raw trial_in_episode

records.content:
Pooling across episodes when fitting an interaction with raw trial_in_episode over-weights late-trial information from longer episodes. Concretely, “trial_in_episode = 20” exists in 24-trial episodes but not in 16-trial ones, and any interaction with trial_in_episode will silently weight late-position information from longer episodes more heavily.

This is resolved by normalisation. All within-episode dynamics models use the normalised within-episode position \tau_t = (\text{trial\_in\_episode}_t - 1) / (L_e - 1) \in [0, 1]. This makes \tau comparable across episodes regardless of length — \tau = 0 always means “first trial of an episode,” \tau = 1 always means “last.”

Just M5 (latent inference at first-c_2 trial) is an exception. It uses the raw trial_in_episode because the absolute count of c_1-trials-since-transition is itself the theoretically meaningful covariate (n_c1_trials_before_first_c2).

hierarchy.lvl4: Risk 2: Episode-length confound with within-episode metrics
Risk 2: Episode-length confound with within-episode metrics

records.content:
Within-episode means of consolidation-style DVs (e.g. circular variance, accuracy) are mechanically lower in longer episodes, simply because more trials are spent at the asymptotic low-variance / low-error end of the consolidation curve.

This is taken into account by including episode_length_c (centred episode length) as a fixed-effect covariate in all within-episode dynamics models.

hierarchy.lvl4: Risk 3: Definition of theta_c2_prev for M5
Risk 3: Definition of theta_c2_prev for M5

records.content:
The reference angle \theta^{(e-1)}_{c_2} is computed as the circular mean of net_mean_deg over the last 3 trials in episode e-1 on which cue c_2 appeared, with fallback to all available c_2 trials in e-1 if fewer than 3 exist. This is documented in 

Dataset Construction. Sensitivity to the choice k \in \{1, 2, 3, 5\} can be assessed by re-running with the --k_prev argument.

Two continuous predictors are centred:

episode_index_c is episode_index minus its per-participant mean. Per-participant centring (rather than grand-mean centring) prevents cross-participant variation in mean episode count from biasing the slope estimate when episode coverage is uneven.

episode_length_c is episode_length minus its grand mean across all trials in the dataset.

Centring matters especially for interaction interpretation (when one term in an interaction is uncentered, the other term’s “main effect” coefficient is its effect at the zero value of the first term, not at the mean. So centring makes interaction coefficients interpretable as effects at the marginal mean)

(This can be a mess. So I included it here explicitly to note how we treat the circular data equally over all analysis steps - this is also a reminder for myself to keep it consistent)

All angular quantities are in degrees. The fundamental operation is wrapping a difference to (-180°, +180°]:def wrap_signed(deg):
    return ((deg + 180.0) % 360.0) - 180.0

This produces the smallest signed difference between two angles. It is applied wherever an angular subtraction occurs:

Computing e_t = \theta_t - a_t (signed angular error).

Computing \Delta_t = \lvert \theta_t - \theta_{t-1} \rvert (trial-to-trial belief shift).

Computing the inference index I_e.

Computing the change-point detection signal.

Computing the recovery distance for the survival event definition.

Circular mean of a set of angles \{a_i\} is computed as:\bar{a} = \arctan2\!\Big(\tfrac{1}{n}\sum_i \sin a_i,\, \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_i \cos a_i\Big),

returning a value in [0°, 360°) via the modulo. Used for net_mean_deg, episode targets in M_SURV, and reference angles for M5 and the change-point baseline.

Defined as 1 - R, where R = \sqrt{\bar{s}^2 + \bar{c}^2} with \bar{s} = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_i \sin a_i, \bar{c} = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_i \cos a_i. Bounded in [0, 1], with 0 meaning perfectly concentrated and 1 meaning uniformly distributed.

Mixed-effects models are fit through a four-tier fallback ladder. The motivation is to increase robustness by also allowing for adaptive complexity increase as soon as more data is available. At small N or with rich random-effect structures, the most-preferred fit may not be identifiable (and the script must progress gracefully rather than crash). Every fit records which tier was actually used in the model’s _diagnostics.txt file.

hierarchy.lvl3: Tier 1: full random-effect structure with REML
Tier 1: full random-effect structure with REML

records.content:
The requested random-effect structure is fit via statsmodels.formula.api.mixedlm with REML estimation. The fit is rejected and a fallback is triggered if either:

The optimiser does not converge (getattr(res, "converged", True) == False).

The random-effect covariance matrix is singular, defined as having any eigenvalue < 10^{-8}. We test on the symmetric part (\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\text{re}} + \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\text{re}}^\top)/2 to suppress numerical asymmetry.

Especially the singularity check is important to avoid understating uncertainty. With a singular cov. matrix, mixedlm will return a “converged” result whose RE covariance is zero or rank-deficient (and naively reporting from it would understate uncertainty).
If Tier 1 fails, the same fixed-effect formula is fit with the random-effect structure simplified to (1 | participant_id). Same convergence and singularity checks apply.

If Tier 2 fails (meaning even the simple random intercept has near-zero variance) the model is fit with OLS and cluster-robust standard errors (CR1, the small-sample-corrected sandwich estimator), clustered on participant_id (

Liang & Zeger, 1986; 

Lin & Wei, 1989, in the survival context):smf.ols(formula, df).fit(
    cov_type="cluster",
    cov_kwds={"groups": df["participant_id"].values},
)

This is the principled fallback when between-participant variance is genuinely near-zero (so a random intercept is unidentified) but trials remain nested within participants (so naive OLS SEs would be wrong). The fixed-effect estimates are unbiased; the SEs are correctly inflated to account for within-participant correlation.
Last resort if even cluster-robust OLS fails to fit (but this is never reached, even with the small piloting N = 17).

The design matrix is constructed via patsy.dmatrices(formula, df) and its rank is checked against the column count. A rank-deficient design (rank < ncol) is recorded as a warning in the diagnostics file (this makes multicollinearity-driven failures legible from the diagnostics output rather than buried in cryptic optimisation warnings).

hierarchy.lvl3: Cluster-robust SEs in the survival framework
Cluster-robust SEs in the survival framework

records.content:
The cluster-robust analogue in M_SURV is the Lin-Wei robust variance estimator (Lin & Wei, 1989), invoked via lifelines.CoxPHFitter with cluster_col="participant_id" and robust=True. This is conceptually parallel to the OLS cluster-robust SEs but adapted for the partial likelihood setting. See 

Temporal Dynamics.

The fallback ladder may end on a fit that has no random-effects machinery (cluster-robust OLS or plain OLS). The _safe_random_effects() helper:

Probes getattr(res, "random_effects", None) (a property on MixedLMResults that does not exist on OLSResults).

If present, returns the BLUPs as a DataFrame, but only if the random-effect covariance is non-singular (per the eigenvalue check).

Returns None otherwise, with the calling code skipping the BLUP write rather than producing misleading per-participant estimates.

A subtlety: hasattr(type(res), "random_effects") returns False on MixedLMResultsWrapper because random_effects is a property defined on the wrapper instance, not the type. The implementation uses getattr(res, "random_effects", None) to handle this correctly.

hierarchy.lvl2: Variance partitioning notes for the binomial model (M7)
Variance partitioning notes for the binomial model (M7)

records.content:
statsmodels does not natively support binomial-family mixedlm. Consequently, M7 uses a tier ladder distinct from M1–M6:

BinomialBayesMixedGLM (variational Bayes), which provides true subject-specific random intercepts (via variational approximation). Posterior means and SDs are returned; for cross-model reporting consistency, we compute Wald-style z-statistics (z = \hat\mu / \hat\sigma) and 95% intervals (\hat\mu \pm 1.96\hat\sigma).

GEE with binomial family and exchangeable working correlation is a population-averaged estimator, used as fallback if the Bayesian VB fit fails.

Plain logistic regression is just the last resort

The Bayesian-VB fit is preferred because it provides participant-level random effects analogous to the BLUPs from M1–M6, allowing M7 to contribute to the 

Factor Structure analysis (GEE estimates are population-averaged and do not yield per-participant random effects)

The framework declares the following confirmatory set of focal coefficients across the seven primary GLMMs:

condition_L main effect in M1, M3, M5, M7

condition_L : oddball_lag1 in M3 and M4 (the focal Factor 1 interactions)

condition_L : episode_index_c in M1, M5, M7

condition_L : transition_lag1 in M2 and M4

condition_L : transition_dir_sum in M5

Across the temporal-dynamics analyses:

condition_L in M_CP_latency

condition_L : episode_index_c in M_CP_latency

condition_L in M_SURV (at both thresholds)

For these confirmatory tests, Holm correction within the confirmatory set is the planned default for analyses on full data. Supporting analyses (other coefficients in the same models, the bootstrap factor-loading stability, the Kaplan–Meier log-rank tests) are reported as exploratory, without further multiple-comparison correction (this split is intended to preserve power on the focal hypotheses without underplaying the descriptive findings - but to be discussed).

The pipeline is designed to be fully reproducible from raw participant CSVs alone:

statistical_analysis.py produces the trial-level and episode-level datasets and runs M1–M7

temporal_dynamics_analysis.py consumes the trial-level dataset and runs M_CP and M_SURV

participant_factor_structure.py consumes the BLUP CSVs and runs the factor analysis

All random-number-using analyses (parallel analysis, bootstrap stability, Kaplan–Meier confidence interval computation) accept seeds for full reproducibility, default seeds are encoded in the scripts.

hierarchy.lvl2: What this chapter is and isn’t
What this chapter is and isn’t

records.content:
I want to use this chapter as a running results documentation, inclunding a standing summary of the analysis pipeline output as it is at the time of writing, intended as the basis for our meetings. Each section reports the effect-size estimate, its precision, and a read of how it compares to the previous batch of data. Where a coefficient was tentatively-significant at lower N and is now non-significant, that is flagged explicitly; where a finding is consolidating, that is noted; where a methodological issue has surfaced, it is described and triaged.

For the model specifications themselves, see 

Primary GLMMs and 

Temporal Dynamics.
These are the effects whose point estimates have remained stable as N has grown from 17 to 27 and whose standard errors have decreased commensurately.

hierarchy.lvl3: 1.1 Instruction depth produces large, monotonic effects on accuracy and defense rate
1.1 Instruction depth produces large, monotonic effects on accuracy and defense rate

records.content:
The two single-coefficient effects with the strongest evidence are both main effects of condition_L (the linear orthogonal-polynomial contrast across the three ordered instruction conditions):

Accuracy (M1)

condition_L β = −0.524 (SE = 0.125, p < 0.0001) on log-error scale. Compared to N = 17 (β = −0.548, SE = 0.144), the point estimate has barely moved while the SE has tightened. On the original error scale, this corresponds to roughly a 50% reduction in mean absolute angular error from condition 1 to condition 3.

Defense rate (M7)

condition_L posterior mean = +0.701 (posterior SD = 0.059) on logit scale. Strengthened relative to N = 17 (PM = +0.582, SD = 0.071); the posterior mean has moved upward and the SD has decreased --> bottom-line performance effect of instruction depth.

The two effects are consistent with each other (better accuracy → more mosquitoes blocked) and represent the most stable signal in the current dataset.

hierarchy.lvl3: 1.2 Within-episode consolidation is universal and substantial
1.2 Within-episode consolidation is universal and substantial

records.content:
The tau (normalised within-episode position) main effects in M1, M3, M6, and M7 are all highly significant in the expected direction:

M1 accuracy: tau β = −0.744, p < 0.0001 (within-episode error reduction)

M3 precision: tau β = −0.402, p = 0.007 (allocation tightening)

M6 placement time: tau β = −0.058, p < 0.0001 (within-episode speedup)

M7 defense rate: tau PM = +0.707, SD = 0.060 (within-episode improvement)

All four within-episode trajectories are consolidating (accuracy improves, allocations tighten, placement gets faster, and defense rate rises within an episode) --> characterises the basic learning curve (and provides face validity for the operationalisation).

Model M4 (trial-to-trial belief shift Δ_t) recovers the canonical behavioural signature of evidence-driven updating:

oddball_lag1 β = +0.949, p < 0.0001 (large belief shift after a single noisy outlier)

transition_lag1 β = +0.696, p = 0.001 (large belief shift after an episode boundary)

omission_lag1 β = −0.440, p = 0.001 (smaller belief shift after an uninformative trial)

The differential oddball − omission contrast is approximately 1.39 log-units (corresponding to a roughly 4-fold ratio in raw Δ_t). My interpretation is that this supports the assumption that participants do approximate Bayesian updating since this effect - that surprise-with-information drives larger updates than surprise-without-information - is exactly what you would predict here.

In M3 (precision), the main effect of oddball_lag1 is robust:

oddball_lag1 β = +0.298 on logit-circ-var scale, p < 0.0001 (allocations broaden after an oddball).

This is a rational response: a participant who has just observed a possibly-anomalous outcome should hedge more on the next trial. The effect is robust and well-precision-estimated.

hierarchy.lvl3: 1.5 Cross-session meta-learning of episode shape is detectable
1.5 Cross-session meta-learning of episode shape is detectable

records.content:
M6 (placement time) reveals two stable consolidation effects and one stable interaction:

tau β = −0.058, p < 0.0001 (within-episode speedup)

episode_index_c β = −0.044, p < 0.0001 (across-session speedup)

tau : episode_index_c β = +0.020, p < 0.0001 (the within-episode speedup “flattens” over the session)

The interaction is logical from my perspective (I think that the within-episode speedup curve becomes less steep as participants gain experience over the session because they are already fast at the start of an episode, so there is less room to speed up further)

hierarchy.lvl3: 1.6 Post-oddball performance is depressed in defense rate
1.6 Post-oddball performance is depressed in defense rate

records.content: In M7, oddball_lag1 PM = −0.555 (SD = 0.114) on logit scale. Defense rate drops on the trial after an oddball, because the post-oddball belief shift is partly mis-calibrated and the participant places nets less optimally for the next mosquito.

hierarchy.lvl3: 1.7 Within-episode signed bias drifts in the anticipatory direction
1.7 Within-episode signed bias drifts in the anticipatory direction

records.content:
M2 now fits with full random effects (it had collapsed to OLS at N = 17). The strongest effect:

tau β = +21.1, p < 0.0001 (signed error grows positively with within-episode position).

Around the centred design point, a participants signed error is approximately 0° at the start of an episode and approximately +10° at the end --> reflects that an anticipatory positive bias toward the next expected target develops within the episode (which is itself evidence of structural prediction/ inference of latent structure (rather than passive learning of the current state))

cue_identity β = −5.19, p = 0.010 (a systematic cue-A vs cue-B angular offset. Expected, controlled-for, not really substantial).

records.content: These are effects that were tentative-to-significant at the pilot N = 17 and have moved toward null at N = 27.

records.content:
This was the cleanest focal Factor 1 finding at N = 17 (β = +0.31, p = 0.025). At N = 27 it stands at:

M3 condition_L : oddball_lag1 β = +0.185, p = 0.135.

The point estimate has dropped by ~40% and the result is no longer significant. The direction is preserved, but the strength of evidence is materially weaker. The companion interaction in M4 (mean-shift) was already null at N = 17 (β = +0.18, p = 0.61) and is essentially zero now (β = −0.007, p = 0.98).

The “ambiguous instructions amplify dispersion-side oddball reactivity” story is not surviving the larger sample. With another 60-70 participants to come we cannot rule it out, but the credible interval at N = 27 is wide and includes zero.

The most “latent inference focused” covariate in M5 (the evidence-dependence of cross-cue generalisation) is now marginally significant. The direction is preserved and theoretically meaningful (more c₁ evidence --> smaller |I_e|) but the strength of evidence is weaker than the pilot suggested.
