All inferential models in 

Primary GLMMs and 

Temporal Dynamics consume one of two derived datasets, both built by statistical_analysis.py:

Trial-level long-format dataset (build_trial_level_dataset) --> one row per trial per participant

Episode-level dataset (build_episode_level_dataset) --> one row per (participant, episode) pair, used by M5

This chapter defines every variable in both datasets, every quantity is reproducible from the raw participant CSVs alone.

Each participant produces a single csv file containing one row per trial. Required columns include trial_index, episode, trial_in_episode, cue_identity, is_oddball, outcome_occurred, actual_vector_angle, instruction_condition, placement_duration_sec, mosquito_blocked, and the per-slot net counts g_0, …, g_31 (or, equivalently, a stringified list all_slot_barriers from which the per-slot counts can be reconstructed).

Two robustness details worth flagging because they bit early in development:

Boolean coercion from CSV strings. Pandas reads csv written Python booleans back as the strings "True" and "False". A naive astype(bool) returns True for both because every non-empty string is truthy. The pipeline uses an explicit _coerce_bool() helper that maps the canonical string forms ("true", "false", "1", "0", "yes", "no", nan) to integer {0, 1}. This is applied wherever boolean semantics matter (is_oddball, outcome_occurred).
Since this was a confusing bug, I thought I’d mention it here.

Per-slot net counts. When g_s columns are absent, they are reconstructed from all_slot_barriers (a stringified Python list of length 32) using ast.literal_eval.
After per-participant loading, the following derived variables are added.

episode_length. for each trial t, the maximum trial_in_episode value in the same episode for that participant. Episodes are jittered in length (16–24 trials per episode by design).

tau is the normalised within-episode position:\tau_t = \frac{\text{trial\_in\_episode}_t - 1}{L_e - 1}, \quad \tau_t \in [0, 1].

\tau = 0 on the first trial of an episode, \tau = 1 on the last. This rescaling is the principled way to handle jittered episode lengths in within-episode dynamics models; see 

Cross Cut Methods.

episode_index is the zero-indexed episode number per participant (episode - episode.min()). This makes the first episode episode_index = 0 and is centred per participant in episode_index_c.

expected_shift_deg is the per-episode ground-truth latent-state shift, encoding the alternating-sign structure:\text{expected\_shift\_deg}_e = \begin{cases}
  \text{NaN} & \text{if } \text{episode\_index}_e = 0, \\
  +120° & \text{if } \text{episode\_index}_e \text{ is odd}, \\
  -120° & \text{if } \text{episode\_index}_e \text{ is even and } > 0.
\end{cases}

This is used by M5 (latent-inference index) and by the survival/change-point analyses’ transition_direction covariate.

The participant’s net allocation on a trial is a vector g_t \in \mathbb{N}^{32} with \sum_s g_{t,s} = 7 (seven nets per trial). From this:

net_mean_deg is the circular mean of the slot-weighted angular distribution. With slot angles \phi_s = 2\pi s / 32 (in radians) and probabilities p_s = g_s / 7:\theta_t = \arctan2\!\Big(\sum_s p_s \sin\phi_s,\, \sum_s p_s \cos\phi_s\Big) \cdot \frac{180°}{\pi} \mod 360°.

circ_var_t is the circular variance of the allocation distribution: 1 - R, where R is the resultant length:R = \sqrt{\Big(\sum_s p_s \sin\phi_s\Big)^2 + \Big(\sum_s p_s \cos\phi_s\Big)^2}, \quad \text{circ\_var}_t = 1 - R.

Bounded in [0, 1]. Lower values indicate tighter concentration of nets around a single angle.

net_entropy is the Shannon entropy of p_s in bits: -\sum_s p_s \log_2 p_s. A coarser, slot-counting-only measure of dispersion that does not respect angular structure (e.g., is insensitive to whether occupied slots are adjacent or opposite). Retained as a secondary measure; see 

Primary GLMMs for why circular variance is preferred as the primary precision DV.

signed_error_deg is the wrapped signed circular distance between the participant’s net mean and the actual attack direction, computed only on trials with delivered outcomes:e_t = \mathrm{wrap}_{[-180°, 180°]}(\theta_t - a_t).

NaN on omission trials.

abs_error_deg. \lvert e_t \rvert, the unsigned angular error in degrees, in [0°, 180°].

delta_t is the trial-to-trial absolute belief shift:\Delta_t = \big\lvert \mathrm{wrap}_{[-180°, 180°]}(\theta_t - \theta_{t-1}) \big\rvert.

Defined for t \geq 2 within each participant’s session; NaN for the first trial. This is the behavioural analogue of belief-update magnitude and is the focal DV in M4 (oddball reactivity).

Three integer indicator variables are derived on each trial, encoded as \{0, 1\} and used both as fixed effects and as bases for lag features:

is_oddball_int (robust bool coercion of is_oddball)

is_omission_int  (1 if outcome_occurred == 0, else 0)

is_transition_int (1 if trial_in_episode == 1 (i.e., this is the first trial of a new episode), else 0)

Three lag-1 indicators capture what happened on the previous trial:

oddball_lag1. 1 if trial t-1 was an oddball.

transition_lag1. 1 if trial t-1 was the first of a new episode.

omission_lag1. 1 if trial t-1 was an omission.

All three default to 0 at trial t = 0 for each participant. Note that transition_lag1 and oddball_lag1 are mutually exclusive in normal data (a transition trial cannot also be an oddball), so collinearity in the design matrix is not a concern.

cum_oddball_in_episode is the running count of oddball trials within the current episode, including the current trial. Included as a control covariate in M4 to absorb the effect of differential oddball exposure across longer vs shorter episodes (a consequence of jittered episode length combined with probabilistic oddball scheduling).
defense_rate for each trial with a delivered outcome (outcome_occurred == 1), 1 if mosquito_blocked else 0. NaN on omission trials.

Two predictors are centred to make interaction coefficients interpretable as effects at the marginal mean rather than at zero:

episode_index_c is the episode_index minus its per-participant mean. Per-participant centring (rather than grand-mean centring) ensures that cross-participant variation in mean episode count does not bias the slope estimate.

episode_length_c is the episode_length minus its grand mean across all trials in the dataset. Used as a control in models that include within-episode dynamics.

For each (participant, episode) pair with \text{episode\_index} \geq 1 (the first episode is excluded because there is no preceding episode to use as a reference), the following are computed and stored:

For episode e:

Identify the cue that appeared on the first trial of episode e — call this c_1.

Define c_2 \in \{1, 2\} as the other cue (c_2 = 3 - c_1).

Locate the first trial in episode e where cue_identity == c_2.

This is the first c_2 trial of episode e. By construction, the participant has seen multiple c_1 trials in episode e but zero c_2 outcomes in episode e at this point. Their net placement is therefore a pure cross-cue generalisation signal.

If no c_2 trial appears in episode e at all, the row is dropped.

hierarchy.lvl3: Reference angle from the previous episode
Reference angle from the previous episode

records.content: theta_c2_prev_deg: the participant’s circular mean of net_mean_deg over the last k = 3 trials of episode e-1 on which cue c_2 appeared. If fewer than 3 such trials exist, all available are used. Sensitivity to k can be assessed by re-running with k \in \{1, 2, 5\}.

Given \theta^{(e-1)}_{c_2} (reference) and \theta^{(e)}_{c_2,\text{first}} (observed first-c_2 placement) and the ground-truth expected_shift_deg:I_e = \mathrm{wrap}_{[-180°, +180°]}\!\big(\theta^{(e)}_{c_2,\text{first}} - \theta^{(e-1)}_{c_2} - \text{expected\_shift\_deg}\big).

Stored in two forms:

I_e_signed_deg which is the signed deviation (informative about direction of deviation from expected location).

I_e_abs_deg. \lvert I_e \rvert, the headline DV for M5. Lower = better generalisation; I_e = 0° corresponds to perfect inference; \lvert I_e \rvert = 120° corresponds to no shift at all (participant placed at the previous-episode location).

The wrap operation is essential (without it, naive subtraction produces wrong values whenever the angular difference crosses the \pm 180° boundary). The alternating-sign treatment in expected_shift_deg is also essential because the expected shift direction depends on episode parity (return-to-baseline vs away-from-baseline transitions).

first_c2_trial_in_episode is the the raw trial_in_episode value of the first-c_2 trial. Used as a covariate in M5.

n_c1_trials_before_first_c2 is the number of c_1 trials in episode e preceding the first c_2 trial. The amount of c_1 evidence accumulated before the generalisation test. Used as a focal covariate in M5.

transition_direction is "away" if expected_shift_deg > 0 (transition into a non-baseline state), "toward_baseline" otherwise.

transition_dir_sum is the sum-coded version: +0.5 for "away", -0.5 for "toward_baseline". Used as a fixed-effect predictor.

episode_index_c is the episode index centred per participant on the episode-level rows.

Both datasets are written to disk as CSVs by main() for downstream re-use, archiving, and inspection:

trial_level_dataset.csv

episode_level_dataset.csv

These files are the canonical input to all subsequent analyses and to 

Temporal Dynamics and 

Factor Structure.

This chapter documents the analysis pipeline for characterising the latent dimensions of inter-individual variation in behaviour. The approach takes the per-participant random-effect estimates (Best Linear Unbiased Predictors, BLUPs) from 

Primary GLMMs and submits them to a factor analysis after standardisation and redundancy pruning.

The pipeline is implemented in participant_factor_structure.py.

Each participant produces a vector of behavioural traits via the random-effect BLUPs from M1, M3, M4, M5, M6, and M7. These traits include things like overall accuracy (M1.Group), oddball reactivity (M4.oddball_lag1), generalisation quality (M5.Group), and so on. The hypothesis is that these traits are driven by a small number of underlying trait-level dimensions. The factor analysis aims to:

Recover these dimensions empirically from the BLUP matrix.

Provide per-participant scores on each dimension.

Enable later cross-validation against the BayesFlow parameter posteriors fit to the same trials. If the GLMMs and the generative model are capturing the same individual differences, the same low-dimensional structure should emerge from both.

Each model that successfully fits with random slopes writes a M{n}_blups.csv file with one row per participant and one column per random-effect term. The factor structure script discovers and loads all such files in the input directory.

A theoretically-motivated subset of BLUPs is retained (not all random effects are included because this would dilute the factor structure with measurement noise); including only those with named theoretical interpretations preserves the signal. The current inclusion list is:

Models that fall to OLS or cluster-robust OLS (and therefore have no extractable random effects, see 

Cross Cutting Methods) contribute NaN columns; these are dropped before standardisation.

Each retained BLUP column is z-scored across participants:\tilde z_{p,k} = \frac{b_{p,k} - \bar{b}_{\cdot,k}}{\mathrm{SD}(b_{\cdot,k})}.

This is essential because BLUPs from different models live on different scales (M5 is in degrees, M4 is on a log scale, M7 is in logit units). Without z-scoring, M5 would dominate any unrotated factor solution purely because of its scale.

The matrix is checked for collinear feature pairs with \lvert r \rvert > 0.95. When such pairs are detected, the lower-priority member of each pair is dropped, where the priority order is fixed to preserve features mapping onto the focal theoretical constructs:PRIORITY = [
    "oddball_reactivity",          # focal Factor 1
    "inference_intercept",          # focal Factor 2
    "inference_learning_rate",
    "accuracy_intercept",
    ...
]

The full priority list appears in participant_factor_structure.py. The motivation for this step is the following: the M4 random-effect covariance (Group-oddball_lag1-transition_lag1) is typically near-singular at small N because the three components share much of the same participant-level variance. The corresponding BLUPs are then near-perfectly correlated (e.g., \lvert r \rvert > 0.99 in pilot data), which makes the correlation matrix singular, breaks KMO and Bartlett, and destabilises the factor solution. Auto-pruning preserves the factor most directly tied to the focal hypothesis (oddball_reactivity) and removes the redundant indices (belief_shift_intercept, transition_reactivity). Thus this approach allows to preserve methodological rigor, regardless of the sample size (and it also allows for a more nuanced and sophisticated analysis with the same code as soon as more data is available).
Three diagnostics are computed and reported:
A near-zero determinant indicates remaining collinearity even after the explicit pruning step. We report \det(\mathbf{R}) in the diagnostics file as a transparent indicator.

KMO (

Kaiser, 1974) is the ratio of the sum of squared correlations to the sum of squared correlations plus the sum of squared partial correlations:\mathrm{KMO} = \frac{\sum_{i \neq j} r_{ij}^2}{\sum_{i \neq j} r_{ij}^2 + \sum_{i \neq j} a_{ij}^2},

where a_{ij} are entries of the anti-image correlation matrix. We compute a_{ij} from the inverse correlation matrix as\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{D}^{-1/2}\, \mathbf{R}^{-1}\, \mathbf{D}^{-1/2}, \quad \mathbf{D} = \mathrm{diag}(\mathbf{R}^{-1}),

following the standard formulation. Per-feature KMO (“MSA”) values are also reported. Conventional interpretation thresholds (Kaiser 1974; Hutcheson & Sofroniou 1999):

KMO

Interpretation

\geq 0.90

marvellous

\geq 0.80

meritorious

\geq 0.70

middling

\geq 0.60

mediocre

< 0.60

unacceptable

Bartlett’s chi-square test (

Bartlett, 1951) tests the null hypothesis that the correlation matrix equals the identity matrix:\chi^2 = -\Big(N - 1 - \tfrac{2p + 5}{6}\Big) \cdot \log\det(\mathbf{R}), \quad \mathrm{df} = \tfrac{p(p-1)}{2}.

A significant result (typically p < 0.05) is a necessary but not sufficient condition for FA to be appropriate.

hierarchy.lvl4: Step 5: Factor count via parallel analysis
Step 5: Factor count via parallel analysis

records.content:
We use Horn’s parallel analysis (

Horn, 1965) to determine the number of factors. The procedure:

Compute the eigenvalues of the observed correlation matrix.

Generate n_iter = 500 random matrices of the same shape with i.i.d. standard-normal entries.

Compute the eigenvalues of each random matrix and take the per-rank 95th percentile across iterations.

Recommended k = the number of observed eigenvalues exceeding the corresponding random-data 95% quantile.

A scree plot (fa_scree_parallel.png) shows both the observed and random-data quantile curves with the Kaiser cutoff (eigenvalue = 1) and the recommended k marked.

Parallel analysis is the methodological consensus choice over the older Kaiser eigenvalue-> 1 criterion or the visual scree elbow, both of which over-extract in noisy data (

Zwick & Velicer, 1986).
The factor analysis is fit at multiple values of k for sensitivity inspection, by default at k = 1, k =  recommended-by-parallel-analysis, k = 2, and \min(k_{\max}, p - 1).

We use principal axis factoring (PAF) for the unrotated loadings:

Initial communalities h^2_j are set to the squared multiple correlations: h^2_j = 1 - 1/[\mathbf{R}^{-1}]_{jj}.

Substitute communalities on the diagonal of \mathbf{R} to form \mathbf{R}^*.

Take the top-k eigen-decomposition of \mathbf{R}^*: \mathbf{L} = \mathbf{V} \mathbf{D}^{1/2}.

Update h^2_j = \sum_{m=1}^{k} L_{jm}^2.

Iterate steps 2–4 until \max_j \lvert \Delta h^2_j \rvert < 10^{-6} or 50 iterations.

We decided to use PAF for this factor analysis pipeline, since it is the standard fitting method when distributional normality of the input is questionable (Fabrigar, Wegener, MacCallum, & Strahan, 1999) and works well with the standardised but possibly non-Gaussian BLUPs.

We use direct oblimin rotation with \gamma = 0 (the quartimin criterion; 

Carroll, 1953). Oblique (rather than orthogonal) rotation is appropriate when there is no theoretical reason to assume the underlying factors are uncorrelated (and in this case, the M4 random-effect covariance structure already shows that they are not).

The rotation is implemented via the gradient-projection algorithm (

Jennrich, 2002). Convergence threshold is 

10-6 on the projected gradient norm; backtracking line search (Armijo condition) controls the step size. Maximum 500 outer iterations.

The returned objects are:

\mathbf{L} (the pattern matrix): L_{jm} = unique loading of feature j on factor m.

\boldsymbol{\Phi} = \mathbf{T}^\top \mathbf{T} (the factor inter-correlation matrix): off-diagonal entries are the correlations between rotated factors.

hierarchy.lvl5: Why we implemented the factor analysis “from scratch”
Why we implemented the factor analysis “from scratch”

records.content: The factor analysis is implemented in-house (numpy + scipy only) rather than using the factor_analyzer Python package. The decision was forced by a known compatibility break between factor_analyzer 0.5.1 and scikit-learn ≥ 1.6 (check_array(force_all_finite=...) was renamed to ensure_all_finite=...). Rather than pinning to outdated dependencies (which could lead to compatibility issues with the dependency requirements for the other analyses), we implement PAF, oblimin rotation, KMO, and Bartlett directly. This also makes the framework self-contained and easier to audit.

Per-participant factor scores are computed via regression method:\hat{\mathbf{F}} = \tilde{\mathbf{Z}}\, \mathbf{R}^{-1}\, \mathbf{S}, \quad \mathbf{S} = \mathbf{L}\, \boldsymbol{\Phi},

where \tilde{\mathbf{Z}} is the standardised feature matrix, \mathbf{R} is the correlation matrix, \mathbf{L} is the pattern matrix, \boldsymbol{\Phi} is the factor inter-correlation matrix (identity if rotation is orthogonal), and \mathbf{S} = \mathbf{L}\boldsymbol{\Phi} is the structure matrix. Saved as fa_factor_scores.csv for the recommended k.

These scores are intended to support downstream analyses, including correlation with BayesFlow parameter posterior means, with state and trait questionnaire scores, and with the change-point and survival analysis outputs. Most notably, the factor scores could be used for cross-validation of the BayesFlow pipeline and the respective estimated parameters.

To quantify uncertainty in the loadings (at the currently small-medium N typical of this study), we bootstrap the loading matrix:

For b = 1, \ldots, B = 200, sample N participants with replacement.

Re-fit the factor model on the bootstrap sample.

Sign-align each factor to the reference solution: if \mathbf{L}^{(b)}_{:,m} \cdot \mathbf{L}^{\text{ref}}_{:,m} < 0, flip the sign of factor m in iteration b.

Compute mean, SD, and 95% bootstrap quantiles of each loading across iterations.

A loading is flagged as stable and substantial (•) in the heatmap if its bootstrap SD < 0.20 and \lvert \hat L_{jm} \rvert > 0.30. Loadings without this marker should not be interpreted at the current N.

(The sign-alignment step is necessary because factor analysis is identified up to sign and column permutation; without alignment, the bootstrap mean and SD are inflated by sign flips that have no substantive meaning.)

If a --behav_data_dir is supplied, the script additionally produces fa_factor_score_validity.png. This is a heatmap of correlations between the factor scores and a small set of model-free per-participant summary statistics (mean angular error, mean circular variance, mean defense rate, mean placement duration, mean \Delta_t). The idea is, that each factor should correlate with theoretically related summaries; if it does not, the factor labelling is unreliable (or it captures another latent structure that might not be meaningful and interpretable in our context).

Code Modules:

statistical_analysis.py implements dataset construction, M1–M7, and supporting machinery.

temporal_dynamics_analysis.py implements M_CP and M_SURV.

participant_factor_structure.py implements the BLUP-based factor structure analysis.

The behavioral data analysis Framework is organised around three layers.

hierarchy.lvl2: What each analysis is trying to learn
What each analysis is trying to learn
What each analysis is trying to learn
What each analysis is trying to learn

records.content:
This base laver contains seven generalised linear mixed models (GLMMs), each targeting a different behavioural dependent variable, all estimated on the trial-level dataset. The models share a common predictor structure (centred ordered-condition contrasts, normalised within-episode position \tau, episode-index covariates, post-event lag features) and differ in their dependent variable and in which interactions are theoretically focal.

The two “main interest” factors that motivated the design appear in M1–M7 as specific coefficients, guiding the model structures in a pricipled manner, allowing for theory-based and hypothesis-driven interpretation of the respective coefficients.

Factor 1 (oddball reactivity) represents the trial-after-oddball change in belief, captured most directly by the condition × oddball_lag1 interactions in M3 (precision/dispersion) and M4 (mean shift). Whether ambiguity about structure modulates the response to noisy outcomes is the leading question.

Factor 2 (latent-state inference via cross-cue generalisation) represents the deviation from the new expected target on the first trial of the second cue of a new episode (after the participant has accumulated c_1 evidence but no c_2 evidence in this episode), captured by M5. This factor supposed to provide a clean behavioural signature of structural inference vs cue-specific learning.

The remaining models (M1 accuracy, M2 post-transition recovery, M6 placement time, M7 defense rate) characterise the broader behavioural envelope and provide context for interpreting the focal effects.

hierarchy.lvl3: Early-phase companion analyses (M1_early, M3_early, M4_early, M7_early)
hierarchy.lvl2: What each analysis is trying to learn

records.content:
Alongside the seven primary models, the framework includes early-phase companion analyses on a restricted sample (the first ~25 trials of each participant’s session, in two cuts: “pre-first-transition” and “with-first-transition”). The theoretical motivation is that the manipulation should affect performance most strongly early in the session when condition 3 participants enter with the generative model already constructed and condition 1/2 participants must construct it from data. By the end of the session, conditions may have converged via data-driven learning in conditions 1/2. The full-session models average across this early-divergence/late-convergence regime, which dilutes the manipulation signature. The early-phase models test directly whether the early-trial divergence pattern holds.

The companion models share a learning-curve predictor structure: DV ~ condition × trial_index_c, with random intercept and slope on trial_index_c. See 

Primary GLMMs for full specifications.

hierarchy.lvl3: Layer B: model-free temporal-dynamics analyses (M_CP, M_SURV)
hierarchy.lvl2: What each analysis is trying to learn

hierarchy.lvl3: Layer B: model-free temporal-dynamics analyses (M_CP, M_SURV)
hierarchy.lvl2: What each analysis is trying to learn

records.content:
Two analyses that use entirely different statistical machinery (offline change-point detection; survival analysis) to probe the same constructs that M1–M7 address parametrically. The aim is convergent evidence; a finding that appears under both the GLMM specification and a statistically independent operationalisation is much more credible than either alone.

M_CP detects change-points in each participant’s trial-by-trial trajectory and asks how the detected change-points align with true episode boundaries. This complements M5 (Factor 2) without depending on cue identity or on the specific definition of I_e.

M_SURV asks how many trials elapse after a transition before the participant’s net mean falls within a tolerance window of the new target. This complements M5 by addressing speed of reaching the new state rather than quality at a specific reference trial.

records.content:
Per-participant best linear unbiased predictors (BLUPs) from M1–M7 are extracted, assembled into a participant × behavioural-feature matrix, and factor-analysed. The aim is to characterise the latent dimensions of inter-individual variation in behaviour and to test whether they map onto the cognitive parameters that the BayesFlow generative-model fits estimate from the same data (see 

The inference agent). This approach provides a behavioural-side validation of the model-side parameter recovery.

A small number of methodological commitments are applied uniformly across the framework. They are documented once in 

Cross Cutting Methods and referenced from the model chapters:

Ordered-factor contrasts on condition (orthogonal polynomial decomposition into linear condition_L and quadratic condition_Q).

Normalised within-episode position \tau \in [0, 1] to handle jittered episode lengths.

Centred episode index (episode_index_c) per participant.

Cluster-robust standard errors (Lin & Wei 1989) as a fall-back when between-participant random-effect variance collapses to near-zero.

A graceful three-tier fallback ladder for mixed-model fitting (full random-slopes → random-intercept-only → cluster-robust OLS), with explicit detection of singular random-effect covariance matrices.

Trial-level data as the unit of analysis wherever possible, rather than per-participant summary statistics.

Circular-data treatment using signed circular distance with \mathrm{wrap}_{[-180, 180]} throughout.

The seven primary models constitute the confirmatory inferential pipeline. Each is a generalised linear mixed model (GLMM) fit on the trial-level dataset (with the exception of M5, which uses the episode-level dataset). All seven share the same three-tier model-fitting fallback ladder, the same ordered-condition contrast coding, and the same circular-data conventions, all documented in 

Cross Cutting Methods.

How the Models map onto the Cognitive Process

records.content: Figure 1:How the seven models map onto the cognitive process.

hierarchy.lvl2: Conceptual Map of the Behavioral Signatures
Conceptual Map of the Behavioral Signatures

records.content:
The primary models capture distinct but complementary behavioral signatures. It is crucial to distinguish between where a participant places their net, how spread out their allocation is, and how much they shift from their previous belief.



Figure 2:Visual distinction between the primary behavioral signatures. Left (M1/M2): Accuracy refers to the distance of the net placement relative to the true target. Right (M3): Precision (circular variance) is equal to Dispersion and captures the spread of the allocation - placing nets in a tight cluster implies high certainty (A), while spreading them out implies uncertainty or hedging (B).

ADD (M4): Belief-shift captures the trial-to-trial movement of the allocation centroid, measuring reactivity to new evidence or surprise.

hierarchy.lvl2: Implementation details - a common predictor structure
Implementation details - a common predictor structure

records.content:
To make cross-model comparison clean, the following predictors appear in (almost) every model:

condition_L + condition_Q represents the orthogonal-polynomial decomposition of the ordered condition. The headline test in every model is on condition_L; condition_Q serves as a non-monotonicity control. See 

Cross Cutting Methods for the contrast values and interpretation.

tau is the within-episode normalised position, in models concerning within-episode dynamics (M1, M3, M6, M7).

episode_index_c is the per-participant centred episode index, in all models that span the session.

episode_length_c is the centred episode length, included as a covariate to absorb the effect of jittered episode duration.

oddball_lag1, transition_lag1, omission_lag1 are post-event indicators, included where the post-event response is theoretically interesting.

Random-effect structure varies by model and is documented per-model below.

Before every model fit, the pipeline runs two routine diagnostic checks on the predictor structure.

Variance Inflation Factors (VIF). For each fitted model, the pipeline computes the VIF for every fixed-effect predictor (after dropping the intercept). VIF for predictor X_k is 1/(1 - R^2_k), where R^2_k comes from regressing X_k on all other fixed-effect predictors. Values above ~5 indicate moderate multicollinearity that may inflate standard errors; values above ~10 indicate severe multicollinearity that destabilises coefficient estimates. The pipeline writes a per-model <model>_vif.csv file and prints any predictor with VIF \geq 5 at fit time. At the current N (28), all primary models pass with VIF < 5 across all predictors.

VIFs operate on the fixed-effect design matrix only and do not account for the random-effect structure. They are most useful for detecting collinearity among chosen predictors (e.g., redundant covariates, near-perfect interactions).

Event uniformity across \tau. Models that estimate post-event effects against an implicit regular-trial baseline (M3, M4, M6) assume that oddball, transition, and omission events are roughly uniformly distributed across within-episode position \tau. If, for example, oddballs cluster at particular within-episode positions, the post-oddball effect would be biased: we would be comparing post-oddball trials (drawn from mid-episode positions) against an average over all regular trials (including early- and late-episode ones). The check_event_uniformity_across_tau routine bins trials by \tau and computes the coefficient of variation (CV) across bins for each event type. CV > 0.5 flags an event type that is unevenly distributed and may need a tau × event_type interaction term in affected models.

At the current N: oddball_rate CV = 0.44, omission_rate CV = 0.48 (both within tolerance); transition_rate CV = 3.0 (uneven by design, since transitions occur exactly at \tau \approx 0, so this is structurally correct and is already absorbed by having transition_lag1 as a separate predictor rather than relying on a uniformly-distributed baseline).

Before the formal models, the pipeline produces a layer of raw descriptive markers that are interpretable without statistical machinery. The general principle is “raw effects → GLMMs → cognitive models” - to start with what anyone can read directly off the data, and then move to abstraction. Three sub-modules are run.



Figure 3:The hierarchical principle of the behavioral data analysis framework.

For each event type (oddball, transition, omission), the pipeline computes per-trial DVs (\lvert e_t \rvert, \text{circ\_var}_t, \Delta_t) averaged over a 1-trial pre-event window and a 3-trial post-event window. The post-minus-pre difference, broken down by condition, directly visualises the event-driven behavioural responses that M3 and M4 estimate parametrically. The figure descriptive_event_markers.png is a 3×3 grid (event × DV) of boxplots showing the post-minus-pre change per participant.

A simple bar plot of mean \Delta_t over trials t+1, t+2, t+3 after each event type (regular, omission, oddball, transition), by condition (descriptive_three_trial_averages.png). This is the raw-data counterpart to M4’s differential-reactivity contrast.

Two condition-independent per-participant markers test whether participants engage the two latent-state rules at all, before asking how instruction modulates them.

Rule 1 (switching/alternation): mean \Delta_t over trials t+1, t+2 after each transition (per participant, averaged across all transitions). Large = the participant reorients in response to the latent state change.

Rule 2 (cue dependence): per-episode mean angular distance between c1 placements and c2 placements, restricted to late-episode trials (\tau \geq 0.5, so participants have had time to learn each cue), then averaged across episodes. If the participant differentiates the two cues, c1 and c2 placements should be in systematically different locations.

Both markers are written per-participant and aggregated by condition (descriptive_rule_markers.png). The point of these is to first establish whether the rules are engaged at all (condition-aggregated) before asking whether instruction modulates them.

M1

Are they accurate?
How close to the true target is the participant placing their net, and does instruction depth help them get closer, faster?

Dependent variable. Absolute angular error \lvert e_t \rvert on log-scale: \log(\lvert e_t\rvert + 1). The log transform is applied to stabilise the right-skewed distribution of angular error (which is bounded below at zero and unbounded above up to 180°). The +1 prevents undefined values when \lvert e_t \rvert = 0.

Sample. Trials with delivered outcomes (outcome_occurred == 1), excluding oddball trials themselves (their error is intrinsic noise, not a measure of inferential accuracy). Post-oddball recovery is captured separately via oddball_lag1.

Fixed effects.log(|e_t| + 1) ~ condition_L + condition_Q
                + tau + episode_index_c
                + cue_identity + oddball_lag1
                + condition_L : tau
                + condition_L : episode_index_c
                + condition_L : oddball_lag1
                + episode_length_c

Random effects. (1 + tau + episode_index_c | participant_id) (participant-level intercept plus random slopes on within-episode position and across-episode index)

Baseline advantage: condition_L tests if explicitly instructed participants start off more accurate on average.

Within-episode learning: condition_L : tau tests if ambiguity slows down the rate of improvement within an episode.

Across-episode learning: condition_L : episode_index_c tests if less-instructed participants gradually catch up over the session (or if the instructed advantage grows).

Disruption Recovery: condition_L : oddball_lag1 tests if high ambiguity (about structural priors) makes participants more vulnerable to error/overreactions following an oddball-event.

M2

How does the behavior around episode transitions look like?

Dependent variable. \lvert e_t \rvert (absolute angular error), in degrees, on the raw scale (no log transform) (note: the Gaussian approximation is acceptable here because the post-transition window is short and the absolute error never approaches the 180° wrap-around).

Sample. Trials within the 3-trial post-transition window: the transition trial itself (is_transition_int == 1), the trial after (transition_lag1 == 1), or two trials after (transition_lag2 == 1). Oddball trials are excluded. The first episode of each participant is excluded because there is no preceding state to transition from.

This restriction is the core design choice. M1 already estimates absolute angular error on the full session; M2 has a distinct theoretical role as it focuses specifically on the post-transition recovery dynamics. The 3-trial window is narrow enough that participants are still in the active reorientation phase, but wide enough to estimate a recovery slope (this was validated based on the 

Temporal Dynamics analyses).

Fixed effects.|e_t| ~ condition_L + condition_Q
        + trials_since_transition
        + condition_L : trials_since_transition
        + transition_dir_signed
        + condition_L : transition_dir_signed
        + cue_identity

where trials_since_transition ∈ {0, 1, 2} encodes how many new-state outcomes the participant has already observed (0 = the transition trial itself, before any new-state evidence; 1 = one outcome observed; 2 = two outcomes observed), and transition_dir_signed ∈ {+1, −1} encodes whether the recent transition was away-from-baseline (+120°, +1) or toward-baseline (−120°, −1).

Random effects. (1 + trials_since_transition | participant_id). The random slope on trials_since_transition is essential because between-participant variation in recovery speed is itself a focal construct (and supplies the participant-level “recovery rate” BLUPs used in the 

Factor Structure analysis).

condition_L is the main effect of instruction depth on the post-transition error at the transition trial itself (before any new-state evidence). Because the transition trial occurs before any new-state evidence is observable, condition is expected to have minimal effect at this baseline. A non-zero coefficient here would suggest condition 3 participants are placing nets in anticipation of the transition before it is confirmed/supported by observed outcomes.

trials_since_transition represents the population-mean recovery slope, where a negative coefficient means error decreases with each new-state outcome, which represents the expected behavioural recovery curve.

condition_L : trials_since_transition is the main test. A negative coefficient means higher conditions have steeper (more negative) recovery slopes, i.e., faster reorientation per new-state outcome.

transition_dir_signed tests for structural asymmetry, e.g. are away-from-baseline and toward-baseline transitions reorientated at the same rate? A non-zero effect here is itself evidence of asymmetric latent-state structure (e.g. baseline being perceptually privileged)

condition_L : transition_dir_signed tests whether the asymmetry depends on instruction depth.

trials_since_transition is treated as a continuous predictor over {0, 1, 2} rather than as a categorical factor. The linearity assumption (recovery proceeds at a constant per-trial rate within the 3-trial window) is empirically supported by the descriptive plots (see 

Current Results).

M3

How uncertain are they?

Dependent variable. \mathrm{logit}(\text{circ\_var}_t), with values clipped to (\varepsilon, 1 - \varepsilon), \varepsilon = 10^{-3}, before the logit transform. The logit transformation is the standard pragmatic choice when the principled distribution (Beta) is not available in the mixed-model implementation: it makes the bounded [0, 1] DV approximately Gaussian.

Sample. All trials (the DV does not require an observed outcome, only an allocation).

Fixed effects.logit(circ_var_t) ~ condition_L + condition_Q
                    + tau + episode_index_c + episode_length_c
                    + oddball_lag1
                    + condition_L : tau
                    + condition_L : episode_index_c
                    + condition_L : oddball_lag1

Random effects. (1 + tau + episode_index_c | participant_id).

condition_L : oddball_lag1 (the focal interaction for Factor 1 in the precision domain. A non-zero coefficient here means instruction depth modulates how a participant’s uncertainty (dispersion of bets) responds to an oddball, which is the appropriate behavioural signature of differences in calibrated uncertainty representations across the conditions.)

The contrast against M4’s condition_L : oddball_lag1 (mean shift) is theoretically meaningful: a well-calibrated Bayesian observer might broaden their posterior after an unexpected observation without strongly shifting its mean, so a dissociation between M3 and M4 on the same interaction is a clean finding about uncertainty calibration vs point-estimate revision.

M4

How do they update?

Dependent variable. \log(\Delta_t + \varepsilon), \varepsilon = 10^{-3}. The log transform stabilises the right-skewed distribution of \Delta_t.

Sample. All trials with \Delta_t \geq 0 (i.e., excluding the first trial per participant, where \Delta_t is undefined).

Fixed effects.log(Δ_t + ε) ~ oddball_lag1 + transition_lag1 + omission_lag1
               + condition_L + condition_Q
               + condition_L : oddball_lag1
               + condition_Q : oddball_lag1
               + condition_L : transition_lag1
               + condition_Q : transition_lag1
               + tau + episode_index_c
               + cum_oddball_in_episode
               + episode_length_c

Random effects. (1 + oddball_lag1 + transition_lag1 | participant_id). The random slopes on oddball_lag1 and transition_lag1 are essential because between-participant variation in reactivity is itself a focal construct (the random-slope BLUPs are the per-participant reactivity scores used in the 

Factor Structure analysis).

oddball_lag1 (average post-oddball belief shift on the log scale)

omission_lag1 (average post-omission belief shift; should be smaller than oddball_lag1 if participants correctly discount uninformative trials)

The differential oddball_lag1 − omission_lag1 is a clean within-model test of evidence-driven (vs surprise-driven) updating.

condition_L : oddball_lag1 (focal test for Factor 1 on mean belief shift, should be read jointly with the corresponding interaction in M3)

cum_oddball_in_episode (controls for the confound between episode length and cumulative oddball exposure)

In addition to the headline coefficients, the model is augmented with a planned-comparison Wald test of the differential reactivity per condition: β_transition_lag1 - β_oddball_lag1, conditional on the L and Q contrast values at each of the three condition levels. The logic is that a participant who correctly discriminates structural change from noise should react more strongly to transitions (which signal a genuine state change) than to oddballs (which signal noise). This is what the differential captures directly, and its modulation by condition is potentially theoretically informative beyond what either marginal coefficient delivers alone.

The contrast at condition c with orthogonal-polynomial values L_c, Q_c is:\delta_c = \big(\beta_{\text{trans}} - \beta_{\text{odd}}\big) + L_c \cdot \big(\beta_{\text{L:trans}} - \beta_{\text{L:odd}}\big) + Q_c \cdot \big(\beta_{\text{Q:trans}} - \beta_{\text{Q:odd}}\big)

A positive \delta_c means transitions drive larger belief shifts than oddballs at condition c, i.e., good signal-vs-noise discrimination. A negative \delta_c means the participant updates more after noise than after structural change, i.e., a failure of discrimination. The test is computed and reported per condition in M4_differential_reactivity.csv.

hierarchy.lvl3: A practical note on random-effect over-parameterisation
A practical note on random-effect over-parameterisation

records.content: In small-N data, the three-component random-effect structure (intercept + two slopes) is over-parameterised, with strong induced collinearity between the random intercept and slopes (because participants who shift more on average also tend to shift more after specific events). When the random-effect covariance is not estimable, the fallback ladder progresses to a simpler structure or to cluster-robust OLS. The model-fitting tier actually used is recorded in M4_diagnostics.txt.

hierarchy.lvl2: M5 - Latent inference at first-c_2 trial (focal Factor 2)
M5 - Latent inference at first-c_2 trial (focal Factor 2)

records.content:
M5

Can they generalize?

Why M5? M1 through M4 measure responses to observed outcomes. M5 is the only model that tests inferential generalization to a never-yet-observed cue (c2). It captures whether the participant has learned the latent binary structure of the environment, allowing them to place the c2 nets correctly before any c2 evidence has appeared.

hierarchy.lvl2: M5 - Latent inference at first-c_2 trial (focal Factor 2)
M5 - Latent inference at first-c_2 trial (focal Factor 2)

records.content:
Dependent variable. \lvert I_e \rvert in degrees, defined in 

Dataset Construction, eq. 

(7). The signed version I_e_signed_deg is also fit as a secondary model.

Sample. All (participant, episode) rows in the episode-level dataset where the first-c_2 trial exists and the previous episode contained at least one c_2 trial. The first episode of each participant is excluded by construction.

Fixed effects.|I_e| ~ condition_L + condition_Q
        + episode_index_c
        + condition_L : episode_index_c
        + condition_Q : episode_index_c
        + transition_dir_sum
        + condition_L : transition_dir_sum
        + n_c1_trials_before_first_c2

Random effects. (1 + episode_index_c + n_c1_trials_before_first_c2 | participant_id).

The random slope on n_c1_trials_before_first_c2 is essential. The fixed-effect slope captures the population mean evidence-dependence, but participants are expected to be heterogeneous on this slope. A rule-understanding participant (especially in condition 3) generalises immediately based on the transition itself plus their understanding of the binary structure, and their \lvert I_e \rvert does not depend on how many c_1 trials happen to precede the first c_2 trial, thus their slope is near zero. A non-rule-understanding participant only generalises when forced by accumulated c_1 evidence and has a strongly negative slope. Without the random slope, the population-mean estimate averages these two sub-populations and is diluted toward zero. With the random slope, the BLUPs basically become per-participant “evidence-dependence” scores, where a slope near zero = immediate generaliser; large negative slope = evidence-driven generaliser.

If the random-slope structure proves over-parameterised at the current N (singular cov_re), the fallback ladder drops it to a simpler structure or to cluster-robust OLS, and the diagnostics record which tier was used.

hierarchy.lvl2: M5 - Latent inference at first-c_2 trial (focal Factor 2)
M5 - Latent inference at first-c_2 trial (focal Factor 2)

records.content:
condition_L (main effect of instruction depth on generalisation quality)

condition_L : episode_index_c (does instruction depth modulate the rate at which generalisation is learned over the session?)

transition_dir_sum (the structural-asymmetry test: do return-to-baseline transitions show a different inference quality than away-from-baseline transitions? An asymmetry here is itself evidence of binary-state structural learning (rather than independent learning of each cue’s contingencies))

n_c1_trials_before_first_c2 (the most theoretically central control covariate. A negative coefficient means: more c_1 evidence accumulated post-transition → smaller \lvert I_e \rvert, i.e., better generalisation. That means, that this is direct mechanistic evidence of inferential generalisation (rather than rote cue-by-cue learning), because pure cue-by-cue learners should show no improvement in c_2 placement as a function of unrelated c_1 evidence)

hierarchy.lvl2: M5 - Latent inference at first-c_2 trial (focal Factor 2)
M5 - Latent inference at first-c_2 trial (focal Factor 2)

records.content:
Alongside the M5 fit, the pipeline produces a three-panel diagnostic figure (M5_floor_diagnostic.png) addressing the hypothesis that some participants (especially in condition 3) generalise immediately on the basis of the latent-state rule, producing \lvert I_e \rvert near zero from very early episodes onward. If this is the case, the fixed-effect slope is diluted because rule-understanders contribute essentially no variation, and the population-mean condition effect is correspondingly attenuated.

The three panels are:

Panel A - Distribution of inference quality: per-participant median \lvert I_e \rvert by condition. Bimodality within condition 3 would suggest a sub-population of immediate-generaliser participants.

Panel B - First-transition \lvert I_e \rvert: at the very first transition (episode_index == 1), before any session-level learning has had a chance to operate. Low values here would directly evidence rule-based immediate generalisation.

Panel C - Evidence-dependence heterogeneity: per-participant within-subject OLS slope of \lvert I_e \rvert on n_c1_trials_before_first_c2. The distribution of these per-participant slopes is basically itself a test of the hypothesis that the condition 3 participants split in two heterogenous cluster. Specificially, a tight cluster around 0 in condition 3 vs scattered negative slopes elsewhere would indicate evidence-independent (immediate) generalisation in the former.

hierarchy.lvl2: M5 - Latent inference at first-c_2 trial (focal Factor 2)
M5 - Latent inference at first-c_2 trial (focal Factor 2)

records.content: Each participant contributes at most \sim 7 first-c_2-trial observations (one per episode after the first). With target N \approx 100, this gives \sim 700 episode-level observations, which is adequate for the fixed-effects of interest and for a random intercept, possibly tight for both random slopes. This is why the fallback ladder was implemented, since this approach allows to determine which random-effect structure is identifiable in the actual data.

hierarchy.lvl2: M5 - Latent inference at first-c_2 trial (focal Factor 2)
M5 - Latent inference at first-c_2 trial (focal Factor 2)

records.content:
Why control for the cue-dissociation pattern seperately?

To test whether participants are doing cue-dissociation at all, separate from how-close-to-target they are.

This check was motivated by a theoretical concern with M5’s interpretation. Specificially, the first-c_2-trial placement could be near the new-c_2-target for two reasons. (a) Cross-cue generalisation: the participant inferred the latent shift from c_1 evidence and applied it to c_2 (the intended interpretation). (b) Cue confusion: the participant does not differentiate c_1 from c_2 and is simply placing at the recent c_1 location, which happens to be near the post-transition c_2 target due to the alternating geometry of the task.

To distinguish (a) from (b), the episode-level dataset includes a second distance: \lvert I_e^{c_1} \rvert, the absolute angular distance between the first-c_2 placement and the participant’s most recent c_1 placement in the previous episode. If the placement reflects genuine cross-cue inference, \lvert I_e \rvert \ll \lvert I_e^{c_1} \rvert (the placement is closer to the predicted new-c_2 target than to the previous c_1 location). If the placement reflects cue confusion, \lvert I_e \rvert \approx \lvert I_e^{c_1} \rvert or \lvert I_e^{c_1} \rvert < \lvert I_e \rvert.

The m5_cue_dissociation_control routine produces:

A paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test of \lvert I_e^{c_1} \rvert > \lvert I_e \rvert, overall and per condition.

A per-participant cue-dissociation rate, which is operationally defined as the proportion of episodes in which the placement is closer to the new c_2 target than to the previous c_1 location. Thus, a rate near 0.5 indicates chance-level placement (no genuine cue dissociation); a rate substantially above 0.5 indicates genuine cross-cue inference.

A two-panel figure (M5_cue_dissociation_control.png) showing the scatter of \lvert I_e \rvert vs \lvert I_e^{c_1} \rvert with the y=x reference line, and the per-participant cue-dissociation rate by condition.

hierarchy.lvl2: M5b - Cue dissociation as a formal binary DV
M5b - Cue dissociation as a formal binary DV

records.content:
M5b

Are participants’s first-c_2 placements demonstrably closer to the new c_2 target than to the previous c_1 location, episode-by-episode, and does instruction depth modulate this?

hierarchy.lvl2: M5b - Cue dissociation as a formal binary DV
M5b - Cue dissociation as a formal binary DV

records.content: The cue-dissociation control above revealed that M5 (on the continuous \lvert I_e \rvert) is partially too noisy to estimate the cue-dissociation effect in a reliable and clean manner because participants’ placements vary in absolute angular distance for many reasons (general placement noise, partial reorientation, oddball-driven dispersion, etc.) that are not specific to cue dissociation. The binary indicator \mathbb{1}\{\lvert I_e \rvert < \lvert I_e^{c_1} \rvert\} is much more robust to general placement noise. Basically it asks only “given the placement, which target is it closer to?”, which is invariant to the absolute scale of the error.

hierarchy.lvl2: M5b - Cue dissociation as a formal binary DV
M5b - Cue dissociation as a formal binary DV

records.content:
Dependent variable. Per episode, \text{closer\_to\_c2\_target}_e \in \{0, 1\}.

Sample. All episode-level rows with both \lvert I_e \rvert and \lvert I_e^{c_1} \rvert defined (one observation per participant per episode after the first).

Fixed effects.closer_to_c2_target ~ condition_L + condition_Q
                      + episode_index_c
                      + condition_L : episode_index_c
                      + condition_Q : episode_index_c
                      + transition_dir_sum
                      + condition_L : transition_dir_sum
                      + n_c1_trials_before_first_c2

Random effects. Random intercept per participant. The episode-level sample is too small for random slopes at the current N.

Link. Logit (binomial). Fit via BinomialBayesMixedGLM with variational Bayes inference (see 

Cross Cutting Methods).

hierarchy.lvl2: M5b - Cue dissociation as a formal binary DV
M5b - Cue dissociation as a formal binary DV

records.content:
condition_L is the main effect of instruction depth on the per-episode log-odds of correct cue dissociation. Reported as an odds ratio (i.e. how much do the odds of correct cue dissociation increase per unit step in L?)

n_c1_trials_before_first_c2 is the central evidence-dependence test. A positive coefficient means accumulated c_1 evidence raises the probability of correct cue dissociation, which is interpreted as direct support for evidence-driven inference (since pure cue-by-cue learners should not benefit from c_1 evidence in their c_2 placement).

condition_L : episode_index_c tests whether the condition gap widens (positive) or narrows (negative) over the session. A positive coefficient would suggest rule-knowers consolidate their advantage; a negative one would suggest data-driven learning closes the gap.

transition_dir_sum and its condition interaction test structural asymmetry between away-from-baseline and return-to-baseline transitions.

hierarchy.lvl3: Why M5b is more informative than M5 regarding the cue dissociation rule
hierarchy.lvl2: M5b - Cue dissociation as a formal binary DV

hierarchy.lvl3: Why M5b is more informative than M5 regarding the cue dissociation rule
hierarchy.lvl2: M5b - Cue dissociation as a formal binary DV

records.content:
M5 on continuous \lvert I_e \rvert confounds three things: (i) genuine cross-cue inference, (ii) placement noise, and (iii) cue confusion (the placement might happen to be near the new c_2 target by virtue of being near the old c_1 target). M5b isolates (i) cleanly by reducing the DV to a binary comparison that is invariant to (ii) and that explicitly contrasts (i) against (iii).
However, M5 and M5b are still formally complementary because M5b tests whether cross-cue inference is happening at all while M5 tests how close to “perfect” it is when it is happening.

Why a seperate classification in rule-appliers vs naive participants?

Because we want to understand whether participants identify the rules even if we do not tell them what (exactly) is going on (condition 1 and 2) and how long it takes them to do so. Furthermore, also participants that are informed about the rules (condition 3) might forget, misunderstand, discard or simply ignore the rules.

The cue-dissociation analyses (M5b plus the floor diagnostic) reveal substantial inter-individual heterogeneity in how participants engage rule 2. To make this heterogeneity tractable, the pipeline includes a per-participant classification step that sorts participants into rule-application classes based on their cue-dissociation behaviour.

A participant’s behaviour is summarised by their per-episode cue-dissociation rate, computed separately for two episode subsets:

Early episodes: episodes 1-3 (the period in which a participant could still be constructing the structural model from data).

Late episodes: episodes 4-8 (after the participant has had time to learn).

Four classes are defined:

Immediate applier: cue-dissociation rate > 0.6 in both early and late subsets. The participant is engaging the rule from session start.

Late applier: cue-dissociation rate > 0.6 in late episodes but \leq 0.6 in early episodes. The participant discovered the rule mid-session.

Non-applier: cue-dissociation rate < 0.5 in late episodes. Worse than chance, even after the full session.

Ambiguous: anything else (e.g., 0.5 \leq rate \leq 0.6 in late episodes).

The 0.6 threshold is above chance (random placement yields ~50% cue-dissociation by construction); the 0.5 floor for non-applier ensures the participant is not just near chance.

For each participant in the immediate or late applier class, the discovery episode is the smallest episode index at which a 2-episode moving window of cue-dissociation crosses 0.6. For immediate appliers, this is 1 by definition; for late appliers, it is the mid-session episode at which the rule is first reliably applied. The distribution of discovery episodes by condition is a temporal signature of when participants figure out (or apply) the structural rule, which is itself theoretically interesting (and connects to mental-health-linked individual differences as a downstream analysis; follows soon).

File

Contents

rule_applier_classification.csv

Per-participant: classification, early/late/overall cue-dissociation rates, rule 1 marker, discovery episode

rule_applier_contingency.csv

Contingency table: class × condition

rule_applier_classification.png

Four-panel figure: (A) class proportions by condition; (B) within-session evolution of cue-dissociation rate by class; (C) discovery episode distribution by condition; (D) two-rule space showing each participant’s rule 1 marker vs rule 2 cue-dissociation rate, coloured by class and shaped by condition



(A) class proportions by condition; (B) within-session evolution of cue-dissociation rate by class; (C) discovery episode distribution by condition; (D) two-rule space showing each participant’s rule 1 marker vs rule 2 cue-dissociation rate, coloured by class and shaped by condition, visualizes whether rule 1 (post-transition belief shift) and rule 2 (cue dissociation) are aspects of a single “rule-knowledge” dimension or genuinely independent traits.

Why check for anticipatory behavior?

Because we want to check whether participants adjust their net placement to the new latent state even if they are still in the old one. This would be a signature of latent state inference.

A question raised during pilot analysis was whether some participants (mainly condition 3) anticipate transitions before they occur, by drifting their placements toward the new expected target in the last few trials of the old episode.

The plot_pre_transition_anticipation routine produces a two-panel figure on the 5 trials immediately preceding each transition.

Panel A - Anticipatory drift toward the next target: signed angular displacement from the old target, projected onto the direction of the upcoming shift. Positive values mean placements drift toward the new target before it is revealed. Different conditions are plotted as separate trajectories. If condition 3 anticipates, this curve should be positive and rising as the transition approaches; if not, it should be flat at zero.

Panel B - Pre-transition error trajectory: per-condition mean \lvert e_t \rvert over the same 5-trial window. If condition 3 participants anticipate, pre-transition error should rise in C3 as the transition approaches (because they are drifting away from the old target).

At the current N, the results do not support the idea that participants anticipate the latent state transitions (C3 has lower pre-transition error throughout the window but does not show systematic drift toward the next target). However, this analysis will also be applied to the rule-appliers (that will become informative at greater N though) based on the observations described in the sections above.

Why another transition reactivity check?

Because this provides a control mechanism for the interpretation of the M2 effects.

M2 measures post-transition recovery using raw \lvert e_t \rvert, which conflates two things: how much was the participant perturbed by the transition, and how good was their placement before the transition. A participant who was tightly placed before the transition is necessarily more perturbed by it (because the new target is \sim 120° away from their old position), while a participant whose placement was already noisy will appear less perturbed.

To control for this, the pipeline includes a baseline-normalised recovery visualisation.

For each transition, the pre-transition baseline is computed as the mean \lvert e_t \rvert over the 3 trials immediately before the transition (in the old episode). The recovery trajectory \lvert e_t \rvert / \text{baseline} is then plotted over the 5 trials following the transition.

A ratio of 1.0 means current error equals the pre-transition baseline; a ratio of 2.0 means error has doubled; a ratio below 1.0 means error is even smaller than baseline (which can happen after a successful reorientation if the participants new-state placement is more accurate than their old-state one).

recovery_relative_to_baseline.png includes to panels showing (A) the recovery ratio trajectory by condition, and (B) the per-participant “peak ratio” (= the largest perturbation each participant experienced relative to their baseline) by condition. Panel B is thus a condition-level summary of relative transition disruption.



This diagnostic clarifies whether condition differences in post-transition recovery are about speed of reorientation (which should manifest as condition differences in the ratio trajectory) or about baseline placement quality (which should show no condition difference in the ratio after the first 1-2 trials).

M6

How fast are the participants?

Dependent variable. \log(\text{placement\_duration\_sec}). Trials with duration outside (0.05, 60.0) seconds are dropped as artefacts (e.g., break overruns).

Sample. All trials with valid placement duration.

Fixed effects.log(duration) ~ condition_L + condition_Q
                + tau + episode_index_c + episode_length_c
                + oddball_lag1 + transition_lag1 + omission_lag1
                + condition_L : tau
                + condition_L : episode_index_c
                + tau : episode_index_c
                + condition_L : oddball_lag1
                + condition_L : transition_lag1

Random effects. (1 + tau + episode_index_c | participant_id).

tau, episode_index_c (within- and across-episode speedup respectively)

tau : episode_index_c (the within-episode consolidation curve changes shape across the session. A positive coefficient means: late in the experiment, the within-episode speedup is less steep than early, because participants are already fast at episode start. Direct behavioural evidence of meta-learning of episode structure)

oddball_lag1, transition_lag1 (post-event slowdown, with conditional effects via the corresponding interactions)

M7

Did they win?

Dependent variable. defense_rate ∈ {0, 1} (whether the mosquito was blocked, on each trial with a delivered outcome (oddball trials excluded))

Sample. Trials with delivered outcomes, excluding oddball trials.

Family. Binomial with logit link.

Fixed effects.defense_rate ~ condition_L + condition_Q
               + tau + episode_index_c + episode_length_c
               + oddball_lag1
               + condition_L : tau
               + condition_L : episode_index_c
               + tau : episode_index_c

M7 uses a different fitting ladder than M1–M6 because statsmodels.mixedlm does not natively support binomial families. The ladder, in order of preference:

BinomialBayesMixedGLM with variational Bayes (statsmodels); provides a true subject-specific random intercept on the binary outcome via variational inference. Returns posterior means and SDs; we compute approximate Wald-style z-statistics and 95% intervals as \hat\beta \pm 1.96 \cdot \text{SD} for reporting consistency with the other models.

GEE with binomial family and exchangeable working correlation (statsmodels); population-averaged estimator, used as fallback if (1) fails to converge.

Plain logistic regression; last resort, no clustering correction

The fit method actually used is recorded in M7_diagnostics.txt.

The Bayesian-VB fit is generally preferable here because it produces participant-level random effects analogous to the BLUPs from M1–M6, allowing M7 to contribute to the 

Factor Structure analysis. GEE estimates are population-averaged and do not yield per-participant random effects.

records.content:
We assume that the first ~15-30 trials of each participant’s session might be qualitatively different from the rest of the session for several specific theoretical reasons. For condition 3 participants (rules explained), the early trials are simply the first chance to apply the generative model they entered the experiment with - to data that, from their perspective, has the structure they were told it has. For conditions 1 and 2 (basic / rules-mentioned), the same trials are when participants are constructing their generative model from scratch, e.g. they don’t yet know there are two cues with different distributions, they don’t yet know about the alternating latent state, they don’t yet know which trials are noise to be discounted.

The strongest theoretical prediction of the instruction manipulation is therefore not (only) that condition 3 participants are better on average over the whole session,- it is that they are better earlier, because they enter with a model that condition 1/2 participants must construct from data. By the end of the session, the conditions may have (partially) converged. That is, condition 1 having figured out the structure from evidence, condition 3 having applied it all along. The condition main effects in the full-session models are then a mixture of “early differences where the manipulation matters” and “late convergence where it does not”, which might partially dilute the manipulation signature (even if interaction effects with the trial/episode index are included, the effect decreases over the experiment as soon as condition 1/2 participants catch up). And the early-phase companion analyses target this head-on by restricting the sample to the early trials and using trial_index (centred within the early-phase sample) as a learning-curve predictor.

records.content:
Two complementary restrictions are run:

Pre-first-transition (suffix _eptr) whcih covers episode 0 only. This is the cleanest “initial model identification” sample since no episode transition to confound interpretation (thus, any belief shifts within this window are pure within-state learning).

With-first-transition (suffix _ewtr) covers the first 25 trials per participant (but this is configurable), which captures both initial model identification and the first latent environmental state transition, which is the moment where the binary-state structure first becomes observable.

The companion models share the same learning-curve predictor structure:DV ~ condition_L + condition_Q
     + trial_index_c
     + condition_L : trial_index_c
     + condition_Q : trial_index_c

with random intercept and (when identifiable) random slope on trial_index_c. Note that trial_index_c is centred within the early-phase sample, not the full session. A coefficient on trial_index_c therefore represents per-trial change in the DV over the early-phase window specifically.

The within-episode dynamics covariates (tau, episode_index_c, episode_length_c) and their interactions are dropped from the early-phase specifications because they are either constant or uninformative within the restricted sample. The lag-1 event indicators (oddball_lag1, transition_lag1, omission_lag1) are retained in M4_early because the event-driven belief shifts are still substantively interesting in the early window.

M1_early: log(|e_t| + 1) ~ condition × trial_index_c. Tests the most direct prediction: do condition 3 participants have lower error early, and do the conditions converge over the early trials?

M3_early: logit(circ_var_t) ~ condition × trial_index_c. Tests whether condition 3 participants concentrate their nets immediately (low circ_var) vs gradual concentration in conditions 1/2.

M4_early: log(Δ_t + ε) ~ condition × trial_index_c + oddball_lag1 + transition_lag1 + omission_lag1. Tests belief-shift magnitude in the early phase, where conditions 1/2 should show larger updates because their generative model is still being constructed.

M7_early: defense_rate ~ condition × trial_index_c (binomial), bottom-line early-phase performance test.

For each early-phase model:

The condition_L main effect represents the average condition effect across the restricted window. Comparing this to the corresponding full-session coefficient is the first test of whether the manipulation is qualitatively early-phase (a substantially larger condition_L in the early-phase model than the full-session model is direct evidence of “dilution” by late-session convergence).

The condition_L : trial_index_c interaction is the quantitative test of convergence. A positive coefficient (on a higher-error DV) means the gap between conditions is closing over the early trials, which means that condition 1 participants are improving faster relative to condition 3. A null or negative coefficient would mean the conditions stay equally separated or diverge further.

A descriptive trajectory plot (early_phase_trajectories.png) accompanies the model fits, showing per-trial means by condition over the first 30 trials.

For M5 only, an additional M5_signed_fixed_effects.csv is produced from a parallel fit with I_e_signed_deg as the DV, for inspection of directional bias in the inference index.

These analyses are conceptually (partially) adjacent to M5 in 

Primary GLMMs (both ask “when does behaviour change relative to ground truth?”) but they use entirely different statistical machinery and rest on different operationalisations. Again, the aim is to provide convergent evidence (a finding that emerges both under the parametric M5 specification and under at least one of the non-parametric / semi-parametric alternatives is much more credible than any alone).

The two analyses are:

Change-point analysis (M_CP) (Layer 5A), which is an offline change-point detection algorithm that is run per participant on the trial-by-trial trajectory. Detected change-points are compared to the true episode episode boundaries.

Time-to-recovery survival analysis (M_SURV) (Layer 5B). Here, for each true transition, the number of trials elapsed until the participant’s net mean falls within a tolerance window of the new target (episode mean for the respective color cue) is treated as a time-to-event outcome with right censoring.

Both are implemented in temporal_dynamics_analysis.py.

For each participant, the per-trial signal is the signed angular distance of the participant’s net mean from a per-participant reference angle (signed difference is wrapped to get the euclidean distance instead of circular one).

We use Pruned Exact Linear Time (PELT) segmentation (Killick, Fearnhead, & Eckley, 2012) implemented in the ruptures Python library (Truong, Oudre, & Vayatis, 2020). PELT minimises a penalised cost function\sum_{k=1}^{K+1} \mathcal{C}\!\big(s_{t_{k-1}+1:t_k}\big) + \beta \cdot K,

over all possible numbers (K) and locations (t_1, \ldots, t_K) of change-points, where \mathcal{C} is a per-segment cost function and \beta is the penalty per added change-point. PELT solves this exactly in \mathcal{O}(n) amortised time under mild conditions (Killick et al., 2012), via dynamic programming with a pruning rule that discards dominated candidate change-points. model='l2' (sum of squared deviations from segment mean) is used as the default cost function.

By default, the penalty is set per participant via the BIC-style heuristic\beta = 3 \cdot \log(T) \cdot \widehat{\sigma}^2,

where T is the participant’s number of trials and \widehat{\sigma}^2 = \mathrm{Var}\!\big(\mathrm{diff}(s)\big) is a noise scale estimated from the first differences of the signal. This adapts the penalty to each participant’s own signal scale and length. Sensitivity to the penalty can be inspected by overriding via --cp_penalty <value>.

Given a participant’s set of detected change-points \{\hat{t}_k\}_{k=1}^{\hat{K}} and the set of true episode-boundary trials \{t^*_j\}_{j=1}^{J} (transitions only, episode 1 has no preceding boundary), four per-participant metrics are computed:

Per-transition signed latency is computed for each true transition t^*_j. This is the signed distance to the nearest detected change-point: \ell_j = \hat{t}_{k^*} - t^*_j, where k^* = \arg\min_k \lvert \hat{t}_k - t^*_j \rvert. Negative values are anticipatory; positive values are reactive.

Per-transition hit indicator is True if \lvert \ell_j \rvert \leq \text{tolerance} (default tolerance: 5 trials).

Per-participant hit rate is the proportion of true transitions correctly detected within tolerance.

False-alarm rate per 100 non-transition trials is the number of detected change-points not matched to any true transition within tolerance, normalised by (T - J) / 100.

Mean unsigned latency is \frac{1}{J}\sum_j \lvert \ell_j \rvert, computed over transitions where a latency is defined (NaN for transitions never matched).

The per-transition signed latency is the unit of analysis for the parametric test. A linear mixed model is fit on the long-format per-transition table:latency ~ condition_L + condition_Q
          + episode_index_c
          + condition_L : episode_index_c
          + condition_Q : episode_index_c
          + transition_dir_sum
          + condition_L : transition_dir_sum

Random effects: (1 + episode_index_c | participant_id).

The structure deliberately mirrors M5 so that interaction signs and magnitudes can be compared coefficient-by-coefficient between the two analyses (for cross-validation). Transitions where no detection occurred (NaN latency) are excluded from this model and tracked separately in the companion model below.

records.content:
Per-transition hit indicators are modelled with a binomial GLMM (logit link), using the same predictor structure as M_CP. The fitting strategy mirrors M7’s: BinomialBayesMixedGLM (variational Bayes) is the preferred fit, with plain logistic regression as a fallback.

This companion model is informative because a participant who fails to detect a transition does not contribute a “long latency” - they contribute no latency. Treating the hit rate separately (rather than imputing latency for misses) can (hopefully) handle this in a pricipled manner.

For each (participant, episode) with episode_index >= 1:

The target angle for episode e is computed as the circular mean of actual_vector_angle over all delivered-outcome trials in episode e. Averaging across cues produces the latent-state-induced reference (rather than a cue-specific reference), which is the relevant target for “did the participant reach the new state?”. Episodes with fewer than 3 delivered outcomes are excluded. #CHECK

The time-to-recovery is the smallest trial_in_episode value \geq 1 such that\big\lvert \mathrm{wrap}_{[-180°, +180°]}\!\big(\theta_t - \theta^{\text{target}}_e\big) \big\rvert \leq \text{threshold}.

If the threshold is never reached within the episode, the observation is right-censored at the episode length.

The event indicator is 1 if the threshold was reached, 0 if right-censored.

The analysis is run at two thresholds: 15° (strict) and 30° (lenient), both reported. The two thresholds are implement to represent two layered questions. The 30° threshold asks “did the participant reach the right region?” while the 15° threshold asks “did the participant consolidate tightly?”. Consistent vs divergent effects between the two thresholds could themselves be informative.

The semiparametric Cox proportional hazards model (Cox, 1972) is fit at each threshold:\lambda(t \mid x) = \lambda_0(t) \exp\!\big(\beta^\top x\big),

where x comprises:condition_L + condition_Q
+ episode_index_c
+ transition_dir_sum
+ condition_L : episode_index_c

Implementation: lifelines.CoxPHFitter (Davidson-Pilon, 2019).

Each participant contributes 7 (8! CHECK) transitions. The Cox partial likelihood treats observations as independent, which understates SEs in the presence of within-participant correlation. We use cluster-robust standard errors (Lin & Wei, 1989) with cluster_col="participant_id". This is a sandwich-type robust variance estimator analogous to those used in GEE, and is the standard alternative when frailty terms are not natively supported (which they are not in lifelines at the time of writing).( (So this is the survival-analysis analogue of the cluster-robust OLS fallback used in the GLMMs in the framework)

In the Cox model, \beta_k > 0 corresponds to a higher hazard, i.e., faster recovery (the recovery event happens sooner). The exponentiated coefficient \exp(\beta_k) is the hazard ratio per unit increase in the covariate.

The central test is condition_L (a positive coefficient means recovery is faster in higher-instruction conditions, on the unit-contrast scale).

Two Kaplan–Meier survival curve plots accompany the Cox model:

By condition (M_SURV_kaplan_meier.png), where survival means “not yet recovered”

By transition direction (M_SURV_kaplan_meier_by_direction.png) separates away vs toward_baseline transitions, with an embedded log-rank test (Mantel, 1966) per panel. This directly addresses / allows to control for potential structural-asymmetry effects (return-to-baseline transitions might be reached faster than away-from-baseline transitions because baseline is the perceptually familiar configuration)

Overall, each addresses a different facet of the same underlying construct (latent-state inference / cross-cue generalisation):



What it asks

Operationalisation

M5

At the focal first-c_2 trial, how close is the participant’s net to the new target?

Parametric, requires defining I_e and the previous-episode reference; cue-specific

M_CP

When does the participant’s trajectory show a discontinuity?

Non-parametric (PELT detection), no cue-specificity

M_SURV

How long until the participant’s net mean reaches the new target?

Semi-parametric (Cox PH), defines an explicit event with censoring

Findings that converge across all three are robust to operationalisation. Dissociations are themselves diagnostic. (e.g., a participant with low M_CP latency (detects transitions quickly) but long M_SURV recovery time (slow to consolidate) has a different cognitive/behavioral profile from one with the opposite pattern, mapping onto separate model parameters in the BayesFlow generative-model fits (h_{\text{switch}} vs \Delta updating rate) again)
