The three instruction conditions are the primary between-subjects manipulation in TAMBI. They vary in how much explicit structural information participants receive about the cue–direction relationships and episode transitions before the task begins.
This manipulation doesn’t just allow the comparison of learning-from-scratch with partial and complete structural knowledge, but also creates a between-condition manipulation of structural priors, representing exposure to different levels of task ambiguity.

Instruction condition is randomly assigned to each participant before the session and determines which set of instruction slides is presented by the PsychoPy script.
Three instruction conditions with different levels of Task Ambiguity, corresponding to different levels of epistemic (teal) and aleatoric (purple) uncertainty.



Figure 1:Three instruction conditions with different levels of Task Ambiguity, corresponding to different levels of epistemic (teal) and aleatoric (purple) uncertainty.

Participants learn:

The visual layout of the task (circular “arena”, 32 slots)

How to place nets (left click) and remove them (right click)

The meaning of the blood counter (performance feedback)

That mosquitoes will attack, and that placing nets in the attack direction will block them with a certain probability depending on the number of nets, stacked in the respective slot.

Participants do not learn the underlying rules of the environment:

That the two cue colors predict different directions

That a systematic angular offset exists between the two cues

That the direction pattern can change over the course of the experiment

That oddballs exist

Instruction slides:







Corresponding computational models: The pure inference models make the same assumption as Condition 1 participants — no prior structural knowledge, all structure inferred from experience.

Participants learn everything in Condition 1, plus:

That there might exist underlying rules which determine the attack direction

Participants do not learn:

That the two cue colors predict different directions

That a systematic angular offset exists between the two cues

That the direction pattern can change over the course of the experiment

That oddballs exist

Instruction slides:









Corresponding computational models: Condition 2 corresponds approximately to models that know a structure exists but must infer its parameters - most closely M3 (full Bayesian inference) and the temporal commitment models M_sticky, M_count, and M_hybrid.

Participants learn everything in Condition 2, plus:

Rule 3 (Cue Offset): The instructions explain that one color consistently predicts attacks from one angular region, while the other color consistently predicts attacks offset by approximately 70° clockwise from the first. An example illustration shows the direction pattern for both colors.

Rule 2 (Episode Rotation): The instructions explain that at irregular intervals (approximately every 20 trials), the entire direction pattern shifts by approximately 120°. Both cues shift simultaneously. The transition is not announced.

The instruction slides for Condition 3 are direction-dependent: the example illustration showing the cue–direction relationship is adapted to match the actual direction pattern in the participant’s trial schedule. This requires knowing the first occurrence of each color cue in the schedule and the corresponding attack direction, which is pre-computed from the schedule file before the session starts.

Instruction slide selection logic for the direction-dependent slide (cond_3c):first_trial_info = self.get_first_trial_directions_per_cue()
# Returns: {color: {'direction': direction_key, 'color': color_name, 'angle': angle}}

key_c = f"{info_c['direction']}_{info_c['color']}"
slide = self.instruction_slides['condition_3']['cond_3c'][key_c]

Available direction keys: O, R_O, R, R_U, U, L_U, L, L_O (compass directions)
Available color names: peach, curry, frog

This generates 8 \times 3 = 24 possible instruction slide variants.

Instruction slides:











Corresponding computational models: Condition 3 corresponds most closely to the rule-knowledge models: M_rule3 (knows \delta_B = 70°), M_rule2 (knows \Delta = 120°, h = 0.05), and M_rule23 (knows both).

Instruction conditions are assigned randomly with automatic balancing across conditions.

Randomization is implemented at the schedule level as well as at the condition level: participants are assigned to condition before the session begins, and the assignment is documented in the results file. Trial schedules are drawn randomly from the pre-generated pool before presenting the condition-specific (and for condition 3 also trial-schedule-specific) instruction slides.

All participants complete the same practice trial regardless of condition. The practice is a free-exploration phase with progressive hints:

If no net has been placed yet: “Try left-clicking on one of the white circles to place a net”

If one net has been placed but not multiple: “Good! Try placing more nets in different positions”

If multiple nets placed but none removed: “Now try right-clicking on a net to remove it”

When at least one net has been placed and one removed: “Perfect! [Space to continue]”

The practice arena shows the standard display but without any cue color (no actual trial is run). The participant’s placement behavior is recorded but not scored. The practice trial ends when the participant presses Space after having both placed and removed at least one net.

At the end of the session, all participants complete a questionnaire that includes a rule awareness check:

Page C (Yes/No): “Did you notice that the direction of the mosquitoes changed in a consistent way depending on the color cue that was shown?”

Page D (text input, if Yes): “How did the direction of the mosquito change depending on which color cue was shown?”

Page E (Yes/No): “Were the instructions at the beginning of the experiment clear and comprehensible?”

Page F (text input, if No): “What specifically was unclear?”

These items allow post-hoc verification of rule awareness across conditions and identification of any participants in Conditions 1 or 2 who spontaneously discovered the structural rules, or any participants in Condition 3 who failed to encode the instructions.

The screen coordinate system uses degrees of visual angle with the origin at the center of the screen. All slot positions are computed analytically from the angle formula:def angle_to_position(self, angle_deg, radius=8):
    angle_rad = math.radians(angle_deg)
    x = radius * math.cos(angle_rad)
    y = radius * math.sin(angle_rad)
    return [x, y]

The 32 slots are positioned at equal angular intervals (11.25° per slot) on the defense perimeter circle. Slot 0 is at 0° (3 o’clock position in standard math coordinates). The slot assignment for a mouse click uses nearest-slot logic:def get_slot_from_position(self, pos):
    min_distance = float('inf')
    closest_slot = None
    for i, slot_pos in enumerate(self.slot_positions):
        distance = math.sqrt((pos[0]-slot_pos[0])**2 + (pos[1]-slot_pos[1])**2)
        if distance < min_distance:
            min_distance = distance
            closest_slot = i
    return closest_slot if min_distance < 1.5 else None

The 1.5° threshold means that clicks far from all slots (e.g., near the center or outside the circle) are not registered.

Net orientation at each slot is tangential to the perimeter circle:net.ori = 90 - angle  # Rotate net to be tangent to circle

The Mosquito Defense Task is a spatial predictive inference paradigm implemented in PsychoPy. On each trial, participants allocate up to 7 defensive nets across 32 evenly spaced angular positions on a circular perimeter, with the goal of intercepting a mosquito that will subsequently attack from a direction governed by the task’s latent structure.

The task is presented with a narrative and visual framing (described in 

Gamification and Game Design) in which the participant is a defender protecting a central resource from mosquito attacks. The spatial, circular structure of the defense problem directly embodies the angular inference problem that is the scientific focus of the study.

The central visual display consists of:

A central light cue circle that changes color each trial to indicate the active cue identity. During the anticipation phase, this cue is visible; during barrier placement, it remains visible.

A defense perimeter circle on which the 32 slots are positioned at equal angular intervals (11.25° per slot).

32 slot indicators - small white circles at each slot position indicating available placement positions.

A blood counter arc - a colored arc on an inner circle indicating the current blood point total (initially 100, updated after each outcome trial).

Net visuals - image-based net stimuli that appear at slot positions when barriers are placed.

Mouse cursor - a small red circle showing the current cursor position, accompanied by a small net image and a counter showing remaining nets.

Each trial consists of four sequential phases:



Figure 3:ADD

The color cue appears at the center of the screen. No net placement is permitted during this phase. The cursor is centered at the start of each trial.

Purpose: The anticipation phase gives participants time to form their prediction about the attack direction and plan their net placement, without allowing pre-commitment.

Duration: Fixed at 4.0 seconds.

Eye tracking marker: ANTICIPATION_START / ANTICIPATION_END

BIOPAC marker: ANTICIPATION_START_EP{episode}

Participants use the mouse to place nets on the perimeter slots. The mouse cursor is visible and interactive during this phase.

Left click: Place one net at the nearest slot to the cursor position.

Right click: Remove one net from the slot nearest the cursor (if the slot contains at least one net).

Maximum nets: 7 per trial. Participants may place fewer; they do not need to use all 7.

Stacking: Multiple nets may be placed in the same slot, up to 7. The defense effectiveness of stacked nets follows a cubic decline function (see 

Outcomes).

Time limit: 7.0 seconds. Visual countdown is not displayed, but audio beeps sound at 3s, 2s, and 1s remaining.

Early advancement: The phase ends early if all 7 nets are placed (though participants may continue repositioning until time expires or they press no further keys).

The cursor display during placement shows: a small net image offset from the cursor, a counter displaying remaining nets (e.g., 5/7), and a hover highlight on the nearest slot.

Slot assignment uses nearest-slot logic: the clicked position maps to whichever of the 32 slots is closest. Slots are positioned at radius 8° from center; clicks anywhere on the screen are mapped to the nearest slot.

Eye tracking marker: PLACEMENT_START / PLACEMENT_END

A jittered delay between net placement and outcome delivery. The color cue remains visible. No new interactions are possible.

Duration: Drawn from a truncated exponential distribution: \text{ISI} \sim \min(\text{Exp}(\lambda = 0.5) + 2.0, 4.0) seconds.

Purpose: Prevents participants from anticipating outcome onset from a fixed delay; adds temporal jitter for physiological data analysis.

Eye tracking marker: ISI_START / ISI_END
The outcome phase varies depending on whether the current trial is an outcome trial or an omission trial.

The mosquito approaches from the attack angle, animated over 2.3 seconds. The mosquito image is selected based on the attack direction (8 directional variants: N, NE, E, SE, S, SW, W, NW). The mosquito buzz sound plays throughout the animation.

Blocked outcome: If the mosquito encounters a net in its approach slot, it reverses and flies back. The show_blood_gain_feedback() function displays a green sparkle animation, “+1 ♥” rising animation, and “BLOCKED!” text. Blood points increase by 1.

Hit outcome: The mosquito reaches the center. The show_blood_loss_feedback() function displays a red flash overlay, screen shake effect, “-1 ♥” falling animation, and “BITE!” text. Blood points decrease by 1.

This function follows a cubic decline: p_{\text{hit}}(n) = 0.5 \cdot \left(\frac{7-n}{6}\right)^3 for n > 0, with p_{\text{hit}}(0) = 1 and p_{\text{hit}}(7) = 0.

Eye tracking markers: OUTCOME_START, MOSQUITO_ANGLE_{angle}, OUTCOME_BLOCKED or OUTCOME_NOT_BLOCKED

No mosquito animation is shown. A brief “...” display appears for 2.3 seconds. No blood points change. No sound plays.

Eye tracking marker: NO_OUTCOME

Participants begin with 100 blood points. Points change by ±1 per outcome trial (no change on omission trials). The blood counter is displayed continuously as:

A numerical value at center screen

A colored arc on the inner circle that shrinks as blood points decrease

A heart symbol (♥) whose color shifts from green (>60 points) to yellow-orange (30–60) to red (<30 points)

The blood counter has no mechanical consequence in the current implementation — it does not end the experiment and does not trigger any qualitative state change. It functions purely as a continuous feedback display of cumulative performance, providing motivational salience to individual trial outcomes.

Scheduled breaks occur approximately every 40–60 trials (randomized to avoid episode boundary overlap). The break screen is displayed until the participant presses Space, after which an experimenter confirmation screen waits for the experimenter to press ‘P’ before the experiment resumes. Maximum break duration is 3 minutes. After each break, a drift correction is performed and a 3-2-1 countdown is shown before the next trial begins.

Within-task emotional state ratings are collected 1–2 times per episode. The rating is triggered at either trial 7 or trial 14 of the episode (randomly chosen per episode), but not both. If not triggered at trial 7 (50% chance), it is triggered at trial 14 by default.

The rating screen presents six continuous sliders (valence, arousal, anxiety, stress, frustration, fatigue) in randomized order. Participants drag the sliders with the mouse. The rating is confirmed by pressing Space.

Trial schedules are pre-generated as CSV files and stored in the trial_schedules/ directory. One schedule file is selected randomly at session start, ensuring that trial properties are rigorously controlled while still providing participant-level randomization. This approach avoids the risk of generating invalid schedules at runtime (e.g., constraint violations) and enables efficient session startup, ensuring low susceptibility to errors.

Each schedule file is generated by the trial_schedule_generation.ipynb notebook and encodes all trial-level information needed by the PsychoPy task: episode membership, cue color, target angle, observed angle (target + von Mises noise), outcome occurrence, and oddball flag.

The following parameters govern schedule generation by imposing specific constraints (e.g., “design‑rules”). This ensures controlled randomization within defined boundaries - in this way, the analysis can focus on the structural parameters of the task, while the constrained randomization (or jittering) ensures that any observed effects are not merely artifacts of a highly specific trial sequence.

Episode lengths are drawn to satisfy:

Each length is in the range [16, 24] trials

The mean across all 9 episodes is in [19, 21] trials

The generation procedure iteratively adjusts lengths until both constraints are satisfied. The jitter ensures that participants cannot use trial counting to predict episode transitions.

The boundary constraint is the most demanding structural requirement: the last trial of each episode and the first trial of the next episode must display the same color cue. This prevents a cue switch from co-occurring with an episode transition, ensuring that the only signal of a transition is the change in attack direction.

A secondary constraint requires that:

Episodes 2–5 each begin with 3–4 consecutive trials of the cue that initiates the learning phase

The learning-phase cue identities are balanced (2 of each identity in episodes 2–5)

All 8 episode transition cues are balanced (4 of each identity)

The planning algorithm proceeds as follows:

Generate a balanced sequence of transition cue identities (4 of identity 1, 4 of identity 2, with the first 4 balanced 2:2).

For each episode, attempt to generate a valid cue sequence satisfying:

Required start color (= last color of previous episode, if available)

Required learning-phase start color (for episodes 2–5)

Maximum within-episode color streak of 3

Approximately equal representation of both colors within the episode

If any episode produces a conflict between the boundary constraint and the learning-phase identity requirement, discard the current identity sequence and retry with a new one.

This procedure is iterated up to 1000 times. In practice, a valid plan is almost always found within ~50 attempts.

After the cue sequence is determined, outcome omission trials are assigned per episode:

Valid positions for omissions exclude:

The first 4 trials of each episode (learning phase)

The last trial of each episode

Trials immediately following a cue switch

Placement algorithm:

Compute target omission count based on a random rate drawn from [5%, 15%]

Select omission positions from valid positions, avoiding consecutive omissions where possible

If strict non-consecutive selection cannot achieve the target count, allow consecutive omissions

Oddballs are assigned after omissions, with additional constraints:

Valid positions for oddballs additionally exclude:

Trials that are already omission trials

Trials immediately adjacent (±1) to omission trials

Consecutive oddball avoidance: If the target count cannot be achieved without consecutive oddballs, the target is reduced rather than allowing consecutive oddballs.

Overlap check: A validation step confirms that no trial is simultaneously an omission and an oddball. The schedule generation notebook reports the overlap count, which must be 0.

For each trial:

The target angle is computed from:

The current episode’s base angle (= initial_start_angle for odd episodes, initial_start_angle + 120° for even episodes)

The cue identity (identity 2 adds rule3_shift = 70° to the base angle)

The actual angle is obtained by adding von Mises noise:\omega_t \sim \mathcal{VM}(\mu_{\text{target}},\, \kappa = 40)

This gives a typical angular deviation of approximately ±10° (SD ≈ 10°, range ≈ 50°, maximum range is constrained to < 60°), matching the concentration parameter used in the simulation models.

For oddball trials, the target angle is first shifted by +120°, then the same noise process is applied.

#============================================================#
# VON MISES VECTOR ENCODING FUNCTION 
#============================================================#

# function to add angle noise 
# Args:
#   - target_angle in degree
#   - noise_std in degree
#   - use_gaussian: True for gaussian noise, false for von Mises based noise
# Returns:
#   - angle with noise in degree (0-360) --> Stimulus angle

def add_angle_noise(target_angle, kappa_noise=kappa, use_gaussian=False):
        
    # Sample from von Mises distribution centred around target_angle
    target_rad = np.radians(target_angle)
    noisy_rad = vonmises.rvs(kappa_noise, loc=target_rad)
    noisy_angle = np.degrees(noisy_rad) % 360
    
    return noisy_angle

#============================================================#
# Function to generate balanced episode lengths
#============================================================#

def generate_balanced_episode_lengths(n_episodes, base_length, min_length, max_length, target_mean_min, target_mean_max):
   
    target_total = base_length * n_episodes  # Target total trials
    
    while True:
        # Generate random lengths within the allowed range
        lengths = []
        for i in range(n_episodes):
            length = random.randint(min_length, max_length)
            lengths.append(length)
        
        # Check if the mean is within acceptable range
        actual_mean = sum(lengths) / n_episodes
        
        if target_mean_min <= actual_mean <= target_mean_max:
            return lengths
        
        # If mean is too low or too high, adjust some episodes
        if actual_mean < target_mean_min:
            # Need to increase some episodes
            deficit = target_total - sum(lengths)
            for i in range(min(deficit, n_episodes)):
                if lengths[i] < max_length:
                    lengths[i] += 1
        elif actual_mean > target_mean_max:
            # Need to decrease some episodes  
            excess = sum(lengths) - target_total
            for i in range(min(excess, n_episodes)):
                if lengths[i] > min_length:
                    lengths[i] -= 1
        
        # Check again
        actual_mean = sum(lengths) / n_episodes
        if target_mean_min <= actual_mean <= target_mean_max:
            return lengths

#====================================================================#
# Function to assign outcome omissions
#====================================================================#

def assign_outcomes_per_episode(trials, min_rate=min_omission_rate, max_rate=max_omission_rate, target_overall=target_overall_omission_rate):
    
    outcomes = np.ones(len(trials))
    
    # Group trials by episode
    episodes = {}
    for i, trial in enumerate(trials):
        ep = trial['episode']
        if ep not in episodes:
            episodes[ep] = []
        episodes[ep].append(i)

    episode_rates = {}
    total_omissions_assigned = 0

    # Schritt 1: Für jede Episode exakte Anzahl basierend auf Episode-Rate bestimmen
    for episode_num, trial_indices in episodes.items():
        episode_length = len(trial_indices)
        
        # Zufällige Rate innerhalb der erlaubten Grenzen
        episode_rate = random.uniform(min_rate, max_rate)
        target_omissions = round(episode_length * episode_rate)
        
        # Strikt innerhalb min/max bounds halten
        min_allowed = max(1, round(episode_length * min_rate))
        max_allowed = round(episode_length * max_rate)
        target_omissions = max(min_allowed, min(max_allowed, target_omissions))

        # Valide Positionen bestimmen
        valid_indices = []
        for i in trial_indices:
            trial = trials[i]
            if trial['trial_in_episode'] <= 4:   # learning phase
                continue
            if trial['trial_in_episode'] == trial['episode_length']:  # last trial
                continue
            if i > 0 and trials[i]['cue_color'] != trials[i-1]['cue_color']:  # cue switch
                continue
            valid_indices.append(i)

        # Anzahl an verfügbare Positionen anpassen falls nötig
        if len(valid_indices) < target_omissions:
            print(f"[Warnung] Episode {episode_num}: nur {len(valid_indices)} gültige Positionen "
                  f"für {target_omissions} gewünschte Omissions")
            target_omissions = len(valid_indices)

        # Deterministische Auswahl ohne consecutive
        random.shuffle(valid_indices)
        selected = []
        for candidate in valid_indices:
            if all(abs(candidate - s) > 1 for s in selected):
                selected.append(candidate)
            if len(selected) >= target_omissions:
                break

        # Falls nicht genug non-consecutive Positionen verfügbar, fülle mit beliebigen auf
        if len(selected) < target_omissions:
            remaining_valid = [idx for idx in valid_indices if idx not in selected]
            needed = target_omissions - len(selected)
            random.shuffle(remaining_valid)
            selected.extend(remaining_valid[:needed])

        # Omissions setzen
        for idx in selected:
            outcomes[idx] = 0

        total_omissions_assigned += len(selected)
        actual_rate = len(selected) / episode_length if episode_length > 0 else 0
        episode_rates[episode_num] = actual_rate

        print(f"Episode {episode_num}: Ziel={target_omissions}, "
              f"gesetzt={len(selected)}, "
              f"Rate={actual_rate:.2%} (min={min_rate:.2%}, max={max_rate:.2%})")

    # Finale Statistiken
    overall_rate = total_omissions_assigned / len(trials)
    print(f"\n[Final] Gesamt: {total_omissions_assigned}/{len(trials)} "
          f"= {overall_rate:.2%}, Ziel={target_overall:.2%}")

    return outcomes, episode_rates, overall_rate

#====================================================================#
# Function to assign oddball trials
#====================================================================#

def assign_oddballs_per_episode(trials, outcomes, min_rate=min_oddball_rate, max_rate=max_oddball_rate, target_overall=oddball_rate):
    
    oddballs = np.zeros(len(trials))
    
    # Group trials by episode
    episodes = {}
    for i, trial in enumerate(trials):
        ep = trial['episode']
        if ep not in episodes:
            episodes[ep] = []
        episodes[ep].append(i)

    episode_targets = {}
    episode_assigned = {}
    episode_rates = {}
    episode_valid_indices = {}
    total_oddballs_assigned = 0

    # Schritt 1: pro Episode grobe Rate bestimmen
    for episode_num, trial_indices in episodes.items():
        episode_length = len(trial_indices)
        episode_rate = random.uniform(min_rate, max_rate)
        target_oddballs = round(episode_length * episode_rate)

        # min/max bounds
        min_possible = max(1, round(episode_length * min_rate))
        max_possible = round(episode_length * max_rate)
        target_oddballs = max(min_possible, min(max_possible, target_oddballs))

        episode_targets[episode_num] = target_oddballs

    # Schritt 2: oddballs tatsächlich zuweisen (ohne globales Balancing)
    for episode_num, trial_indices in episodes.items():
        episode_length = len(trial_indices)
        
        # Zufällige Rate innerhalb der erlaubten Grenzen
        episode_rate = random.uniform(min_rate, max_rate)
        target_oddballs = round(episode_length * episode_rate)
        
        # Strikt innerhalb min/max bounds halten
        min_allowed = max(1, round(episode_length * min_rate))
        max_allowed = round(episode_length * max_rate)
        target_oddballs = max(min_allowed, min(max_allowed, target_oddballs))

        # valide Positionen bestimmen (gleiche Restriktionen wie outcomes + keine outcome omissions + nicht vor outcome omissions)
        valid_indices = []
        for i in trial_indices:
            trial = trials[i]
            if trial['trial_in_episode'] <= 4:   # learning phase
                continue
            if trial['trial_in_episode'] == trial['episode_length']:  # letztes Trial
                continue
            if i > 0 and trials[i]['cue_color'] != trials[i-1]['cue_color']:  # cue switch
                continue
            if outcomes[i] == 0:  # bereits outcome omission
                continue
            if i+1 < len(outcomes) and outcomes[i+1] == 0:  # nächstes Trial ist outcome omission
                continue
            if i-1 >= 0 and outcomes[i-1] == 0:  # vorheriges Trial ist outcome omission
                continue
            valid_indices.append(i)

        if len(valid_indices) < target_oddballs:
            print(f"[Warnung] Episode {episode_num}: nur {len(valid_indices)} gültige Positionen "
                  f"für {target_oddballs} gewünschte Oddballs")
            target_oddballs = len(valid_indices)

        # deterministische Auswahl ohne consecutive
        random.shuffle(valid_indices)
        selected = []
        for candidate in valid_indices:
            if all(abs(candidate - s) > 1 for s in selected):
                selected.append(candidate)
            if len(selected) >= target_oddballs:
                break

        # Falls nicht genug non-consecutive Positionen verfügbar, versuche weniger strikt
        if len(selected) < target_oddballs:
            remaining_valid = [idx for idx in valid_indices if idx not in selected]
            needed = target_oddballs - len(selected)
            
            # Versuche erst noch non-consecutive zu finden
            for candidate in remaining_valid:
                if all(abs(candidate - s) > 1 for s in selected) and len(selected) < target_oddballs:
                    selected.append(candidate)
            
            # Falls immer noch nicht genug, reduziere das Ziel anstatt consecutive zu erlauben
            if len(selected) < target_oddballs:
                print(f"[Info] Episode {episode_num}: Reduziere Oddball-Ziel von {target_oddballs} auf {len(selected)} "
                      f"um consecutive Oddballs zu vermeiden")
                target_oddballs = len(selected)

        for idx in selected:
            oddballs[idx] = 1

        total_oddballs_assigned += len(selected)
        episode_assigned[episode_num] = len(selected)
        actual_rate = len(selected) / episode_length if episode_length > 0 else 0
        episode_rates[episode_num] = actual_rate

        print(f"Episode {episode_num}: Oddball Ziel={target_oddballs}, "
              f"gesetzt={len(selected)}, "
              f"Rate={actual_rate:.2%} (min={min_rate:.2%}, max={max_rate:.2%})")

    # Finale Statistiken ohne globales Balancing
    total_oddballs = int(np.sum(oddballs))
    overall_rate = total_oddballs / len(trials)

    print(f"\n[Final Oddball] Gesamt: {total_oddballs}/{len(trials)} "
          f"= {overall_rate:.2%}, Ziel={target_overall:.2%}")

    return oddballs, episode_rates, overall_rate

#===============================================================#
# Function to generate cue sequence for an episode
#===============================================================#

def generate_valid_cue_sequence(n_trials, required_start_color=None, required_end_color=None, episode_num=1, learning_phase_trials=0):
    
    for attempt in range(max_sequence_attempts):
        # Calculate number of trials per color (approximately half each -> 10 +/- 2 on average)
        n_color1 = random.randint(max(1, n_trials//2 - 2), min(n_trials-1, n_trials//2 + 2))
        n_color2 = n_trials - n_color1
        
        counts = {cue_colors[0]: 0, cue_colors[1]: 0}
        sequence = []
        
        # Handle learning phase trials at the start
        learning_trials_added = 0
        if learning_phase_trials > 0 and required_start_color:
            for i in range(min(learning_phase_trials, n_trials)):
                sequence.append(required_start_color)
                counts[required_start_color] += 1
                learning_trials_added += 1
        
        # Fill remaining positions
        remaining_positions = n_trials - learning_trials_added
        if required_end_color and remaining_positions > 0:
            remaining_positions -= 1  
        
        # Calculate remaining quota for each color
        remaining_color1 = n_color1 - counts[cue_colors[0]]
        remaining_color2 = n_color2 - counts[cue_colors[1]]
        
        # Adjust if end color requirement affects quota
        if required_end_color:
            if required_end_color == cue_colors[0]:
                remaining_color1 -= 1
            else:
                remaining_color2 -= 1
        
        # Generate middle section randomly while respecting quotas and streak limits
        temp_counts = {cue_colors[0]: remaining_color1, cue_colors[1]: remaining_color2}
        
        for pos in range(remaining_positions):
            available_colors = [c for c in cue_colors if temp_counts[c] > 0]
            
            # Enforce max streak constraint
            if len(sequence) >= max_same_color_streak:
                if all(color == sequence[-1] for color in sequence[-max_same_color_streak:]):
                    if sequence[-1] in available_colors:
                        available_colors.remove(sequence[-1])
            
            if not available_colors:
                break  # Failed this attempt, try again
            
            chosen_color = random.choice(available_colors)
            sequence.append(chosen_color)
            temp_counts[chosen_color] -= 1
        
        # Add required end color if specified
        if required_end_color and len(sequence) == n_trials - 1:
            sequence.append(required_end_color)
            counts[required_end_color] += 1
        
        # Verify the sequence meets all requirements
        if (len(sequence) == n_trials and 
            sequence.count(cue_colors[0]) == n_color1 and 
            sequence.count(cue_colors[1]) == n_color2 and
            (not required_start_color or sequence[0] == required_start_color) and
            (not required_end_color or sequence[-1] == required_end_color)):
            return sequence
    
    raise Exception(f"Could not generate valid sequence for episode {episode_num} after {max_sequence_attempts} attempts")

        for episode in range(1, n_episodes + 1):
            episode_length = episode_lengths[episode - 1]
            
            # CRITICAL: Boundary constraint is ALWAYS enforced - no exceptions
            required_start_color = prev_last_color  # Must match previous episode's end
            required_end_color = None  # Will be determined by the sequence generation
            
            # Learning phase: episodes 2-5 need 3-4 consecutive trials of specific identity
            learning_phase_trials = 0

            if 2 <= episode <= 5:

                # CHECK: Does learning phase requirement conflict with boundary constraint?
                if required_start_color is not None and required_start_color != required_learning_color:
                    # CONFLICT! This episode assignment won't work - need to retry with different assignments
                    raise Exception(f"Conflict in episode {episode}: boundary needs {required_start_color}, learning needs {required_learning_color}")
                
                # If no conflict, use learning phase requirement
                if required_start_color is None:  # Episode 2
                    required_start_color = required_learning_color
                
                learning_phase_trials = random.randint(min_learning_phase_trials, max_learning_phase_trials)

            sequence = generate_valid_cue_sequence(

                learning_phase_trials=learning_phase_trials
            )

            if episode >= 2:

        if not all_8_balanced:
            raise Exception(f"All 8 episodes not balanced: {all_8_count_1} vs {all_8_count_2}")

    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Planning attempt {planning_attempt + 1} failed: {e}")

        all_8_identities = [1, 1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2, 2]

            balance_attempts += 1
            
        episode_cue_identities = all_8_identities
        print(f"Retrying with new identity sequence: {episode_cue_identities}")
        continue
